content.text:
Prerequisite: Experiment 1 validated and committed. Branch off it: git checkout experiment/decorations-1-additive-indent && git checkout -b experiment/decorations-2b-guides-css-gradient. Read the same two docs as 2a first — this is a deliberate head-to-head alternative to 2a, not a fallback; build both, don't assume a winner in advance. 

Build guide lines as a single Decoration.line per line, with one CSS custom property set to a comma-joined list of repeating-linear-gradient(...) layers (one per ancestor level), consumed by a single ::before — this is the technique @replit/codemirror-indentation-markers uses (its src/index.ts is the reference if you want the exact shape): O(1) DOM nodes per line regardless of depth, no JS pixel measurement, no overlay layer. Because this reintroduces a real CSS rule participating in the normal cascade (unlike 2a's inline-styled overlay), explicitly verify it does NOT resurrect any of the specificity/!important problems from the original postmortem — it shouldn't, since guide-drawing isn't a property any native rule already contests, but confirm rather than assume.

Same fixture corpus, same both-themes requirement, same multi-line-continuation focus as 2a (get the line's own rendered full height for the gradient — should be free since it's one element per whole line, but verify). Record results in the comparison table, specifically comparing code size/complexity and robustness against 2a.

1	# Postmortem: the `outline-decorations` change (2026-07-13)
2	
3	**Verdict: failed experiment.** After three full redesign cycles, extensive automated
4	testing (198 unit tests, 33 e2e tests, all green), and multiple rounds of screenshot
5	verification, the feature was still visibly broken in real vault use: marker size
6	scaling with heading font-size, inconsistent indentation, wrong continuation-line
7	indentation, markers overlapping native bullets, misplaced guide lines. This document
8	records what was tried, why each attempt failed, and — the more important part — why
9	the testing approach used throughout kept producing false confidence. It's written for
10	whoever plans the next iteration (possibly a future session of the author of this one).
11	
12	## What the change was trying to do
13	
14	Give outline mode a visual language: headings, paragraphs, list items, and code/table/
15	quote/callout blocks should all read as nodes in one tree, with indentation that reflects
16	tree depth (not raw markdown encoding) and a marker prominent enough to make the
17	structure legible at a glance — motivated by a dev-vault finding that flat, paragraph-
18	heavy documents gave no visual signal that outline mode was even active. See
19	[04-open-questions.md](04-open-questions.md)'s "Visual layer is now the testability
20	bottleneck" note and the archived `outline-decorations` OpenSpec change for the original
21	ask.
22	
23	## Chronological account
24	
25	### Attempt 1: paragraph-only decoration, guide lines via fixed-position CSS
26	
27	Scope: only paragraph nodes got a `::before` marker; a separate `::before` line drew a
28	vertical "guide" at a fixed screen offset. List items, headings, and atoms were left
29	completely untouched.
30	
31	Shipped, all tasks marked done, verified via synthetic e2e fixtures (clean headings/
32	lists/paragraphs, no checkboxes) and two screenshots. Two real bugs were caught during
33	that verification round (both by screenshot, not by the DOM-attribute e2e assertions,
34	which passed the whole time):
35	
36	- The `padding-left` CSS rule needed a more specific selector *and* `!important` to take
37	  effect at all — a plain `.to-node-line { padding-left: ... }` was silently overridden
38	  by Obsidian's own later, equal-specificity `.cm-line` rules, even on lines with no
39	  competing native padding of their own.
40	- The rule read `var(--list-indent, 2em)` for the per-depth unit. CSS custom properties
41	  resolve at the point of *use*, not declaration — so the `em` resolved against each
42	  line's own font-size, and a heading (larger font) indented more per depth-level than a
43	  paragraph at the identical tree depth. Fixed with a plugin-local fixed `rem` constant.
44	
45	Both fixes were verified by re-screenshotting the *same two synthetic fixtures* and
46	declared done. Nothing with checkboxes, real nesting, or code blocks was tried yet.
47	
48	### User report: broken in the real vault
49	
50	Two problems, both invisible in the synthetic fixtures:
51	
52	1. `styles.css` wasn't even loading — `vault:install` symlinked `manifest.json` and
53	   `main.js` but not `styles.css`. Fixed, but this alone should have been a signal that
54	   the verification loop (screenshot the sandboxed e2e vault) was disconnected from what
55	   the user was actually running.
56	2. Once loaded: indentation "off, sometimes negative"; guide lines "all over the place,"
57	   including apparently when outline mode was off.
58	
59	### Attempt 2: unilateral pivot to "leave list items alone entirely"
60	
61	Root cause of "negative" indentation: Obsidian pairs a negative `text-indent` with
62	`padding-left` to hang a list item's bullet outside the text column. The `!important`
63	override touched only `padding-left`, leaving the native (smaller, per-raw-level)
64	`text-indent` in place — the mismatched pair put content in an arbitrary, sometimes
65	overlapping position. The guide line's fixed screen offset (attempt 1) also never scaled
66	with depth, so at real nesting it landed nowhere near the actual text.
67	
68	Response: stopped decorating list items, headings, and atoms altogether — only
69	paragraphs got padding or a marker — and dropped the guide-line feature entirely.
70	
71	**This was the first process failure of the session**: a scope reduction (dropping the
72	whole point of the change — one visual language across kinds) made and shipped without
73	checking with the user first. Called out directly: *"how come you decided to change the
74	design mid-implementation and didn't even stop to check with me?"*
75	
76	### User rejects the narrowed scope; redirects to a specific reference
77	
78	Explicit requirements: indentation must be *consistent relative to text* across every
79	block type; a *prominent, uniform "fat bullet"* marker (Logseq-style) across kinds, with
80	per-kind glyph variation an acceptable later refinement, not a blocker. Pointed at
81	`obsidian-outliner`'s `BetterListsStyles`/Vertical Indentation Lines as a concrete
82	reference implementation to study before writing more code.
83	
84	Research via DeepWiki on `vslinko/obsidian-outliner` surfaced the actual technique, which
85	this session had not been using:
86	
87	- Guide lines are **not** CSS. A `ViewPlugin` calls `view.coordsAtPos()`/`lineBlockAt()`
88	  to read *already-rendered* pixel positions (after Obsidian's own list math has run),
89	  then draws absolutely-positioned overlay `<div>`s at those measured coordinates. It
90	  never fights the box model — it measures the result and draws on top of it.
91	- Bullet restyling targets the **existing** native bullet DOM element
92	  (`.list-bullet::after`) rather than adding a new one, and is list-only — the plugin
93	  never needs to make a heading or paragraph imitate a list bullet, because vanilla
94	  Obsidian never indents those regardless of depth. That "make non-list kinds match list
95	  indentation" problem is unique to this project's universal-tree model; there was no
96	  existing-plugin precedent to lift for it.
97	- Its own native indent-guide dashes are explicitly disabled (`content: none` on
98	  `.cm-indent::before`) via a mode class when its custom guides are on, to avoid double
99	  rendering.
100	
101	Given the choice between "make everything match native list styling" and "make list
102	styling match everything else" (author's framing), the answer was explicit: unify by
103	owning the scheme, not by trying to reverse-engineer Obsidian's per-theme list metrics.
104	
105	### The `!important` warning already on file
106	
107	`docs/research/02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md` documents a case study
108	(`workflowy-style-outline`) whose from-scratch UI shipped **~890 uses of `!important`**
109	fighting Obsidian's own CSS, flagged as the majority of its community-scorecard lint
110	violations. This was already in the project's own research before this session started
111	and should have been weighed *before* reaching for `!important` at all, not discovered
112	as a justification after the fact. It turned out to matter far sooner than the "add a
113	settings UI later" scale the case study implies: this session needed `!important` for
114	literally three properties (`padding-left`, `margin-left`, `text-indent`) and still
115	produced a chain of regressions.
116	
117	### Attempt 3: unify all kinds via Obsidian's own hang mechanism
118	
119	Design: instead of fighting the `text-indent`/`padding-left` pair, own both halves
120	together, uniformly, for every kind — `padding-left: depth × unit`,
121	`text-indent: -1 × hang`, applied as one coherent rule to every decorated line (not just
122	paragraphs). A synthetic `::before` marker on kinds without a native one rides the same
123	hang naturally (pseudo-elements participate in `text-indent`'s first-line shift, so no
124	absolute-position math needed there). List items keep their native marker glyph and
125	position; only its `font-size`/`font-weight` are touched.
126	
127	First verification round found nothing wrong — because it was run against a stale
128	plugin bundle. **`npm run build` only runs `tsc --noEmit`; it does not invoke esbuild.**
129	The actual `main.js` Obsidian loads still had the previous (paragraph-only) code. This
130	was caught only by directly inspecting computed styles and noticing list items carried
131	none of the new CSS classes at all — a second, independent build-pipeline mistake
132	(the same category of "test wasn't actually testing the current code" failure, just at
133	the build layer instead of the vault layer).
134	
135	After an actual rebuild, computed-style checks showed correct, consistent per-depth
136	padding (0/24/48/72px) across headings, paragraphs, and list items. Screenshots of a
137	mixed journal fixture (checkboxes, nested lists) looked right — except code blocks
138	still didn't visibly indent.
139	
140	**Root cause**: `padding-left` shifts an element's own *content*; it never moves the
141	element's own border/background box. Invisible for plain text (no visible box to reveal
142	the gap), but a code fence renders a background box whose edges are unaffected by
143	padding — confirmed via `getBoundingClientRect()`: the background box's `left` was
144	identical to its container's `left` regardless of the line's computed `padding-left`.
145	Fix: atoms (code/table/quote/callout/html/hr) get `margin-left` instead, which actually
146	moves the box.
147	
148	### Attempt 3b: unifying bullet *shape*, not just size
149	
150	Restyling the native bullet glyph in place (bigger font-size on the existing "-"
151	character) left bullets looking like bold dashes next to round "●" markers on paragraphs
152	and headings — a shape mismatch. Attempted fix: hide the native dash
153	(`color: transparent`) and draw a matching "●" via `::after`, positioned absolutely.
154	
155	This collided with a native Obsidian element never previously accounted for: the
156	**fold-indicator** (collapse chevron), rendered for any list item that has children. Its
157	wrapper element has zero width in normal flow, but its child icon (~31px) visually
158	extends past that zero-width anchor regardless of the surrounding indentation scheme —
159	so it always intrudes on whatever renders immediately after it. Verified by directly
160	measuring both elements' `getBoundingClientRect()`s and confirming the ~9px overlap
161	persisted even after changing the hang unit — because the fold-indicator's icon size is
162	independent of `--to-hang`; shrinking the hang just moved both anchors together, not
163	their relative overlap.
164	
165	Attempted fix: a fixed `left` offset on the `::after` dot to clear the fold-indicator.
166	This **made it worse** — the offset needed to clear the icon pushed the dot far enough
167	right to overlap the item's own text (visibly cutting off the first letter: "users"
168	rendered as "●sers"). The dot and the text both anchor near the same point; a flat pixel
169	offset that clears one native element pushes into the next thing in flow, because the
170	underlying mechanism (inline flow position, not independent absolute placement) doesn't
171	allow moving one without the other.
172	
173	Reverted to the safer, non-repositioning version: resize the *existing* glyph in place,
174	touch nothing about its position. This can't collide with the fold-indicator or overlap
175	text because it never moves anything — it accepts "bullets read as bold dashes, not
176	matching dots" as a known, deferred cosmetic gap (which the user had already flagged as
177	acceptable to defer, distinct from the indentation-consistency requirement which was
178	not negotiable).
179	
180	### Final user report: still broken
181	
182	Despite the above — full green test suite, multiple rounds of screenshot verification
183	against increasingly realistic fixtures (checkboxes, deep nesting, mixed ordered/bullet
184	lists, code fences) — real use in the actual vault still showed:
185	
186	- **Bullets get bigger depending on the heading size.** A real, self-evident bug this
187	  session never caught: the marker's `font-size` was set in `em` (`0.6em` for the
188	  synthetic marker, `1.15em` for the resized native glyph) — the *exact same class of
189	  mistake* already identified and fixed for *indentation* (em resolves against each
190	  line's own font-size) was never applied to the *marker size* rule. A heading's larger
191	  base font inflates its marker proportionally; a paragraph's or a deeply nested list
192	  item's marker stays small. This should have been caught by the same reasoning that
193	  caught the first em/rem bug — it wasn't, because that fix was verified narrowly
194	  (indentation values only) rather than by re-auditing every other `em`-based rule in
195	  the same stylesheet for the same class of bug.
196	- **Indentation still "all over the place"** in real use, despite computed-style checks
197	  showing correct, consistent depth-based padding in the tested fixtures. Either the
198	  verified fixtures didn't cover the actual failure modes present in a real, long-lived
199	  vault (different note shapes, different nesting patterns, interaction with the user's
200	  actual theme), or there are further interactions with Obsidian's own CSS not yet
201	  identified.
202	- **List node continuations (Shift+Enter multi-line nodes) don't get the right
203	  indentation.** Never actually screenshotted or visually checked — the implementation
204	  assumption (continuation lines get the same `padding-left` as their node's first line,
205	  just without the `text-indent` hang) was asserted in code and in the unit tests'
206	  line-fact computation, but never verified against a real rendered multi-line list item
207	  or paragraph. A real, plausible gap in coverage, not a confirmed root cause.
208	- **List markers drawn on top of the dashes.** This directly contradicts the last known
209	  implementation state (resize-in-place, no overlay `::after`) and was never
210	  independently investigated before this document was written — the two live
211	  possibilities are (a) the user was testing against a stale build (this session's
212	  build pipeline had already caused two prior false-negative verification rounds for
213	  exactly this reason), or (b) a real decoration-application bug — e.g., stale/duplicate
214	  entries surviving across a `DecorationSet` recompute or a toggle-off/on cycle — that
215	  was never looked into.
216	- **Guide lines completely misplaced.** The vertical guide-line feature was dropped
217	  after attempt 2 and never reintroduced in this session's final implementation, so this
218	  report likely refers to the marker/indentation confusion above rather than a literal
219	  guide line — but it wasn't reconciled with the user before this document was written.
220	
221	## Why the testing approach kept producing false confidence
222	
223	This is the most important section for planning the next attempt — the bugs above are
224	specific to a CSS-override strategy, but the *verification gaps* are a repeatable
225	pattern that will recur under a different implementation strategy too if not addressed
226	directly.
227	
228	1. **DOM-attribute assertions test that code ran, not that it looks right.** The e2e
229	   suite checked CSS class names and the `--to-depth` custom property were present and
230	   correctly valued — and passed at every stage, including stages where the rendered
231	   result was visibly broken. Presence of an attribute says nothing about the resulting
232	   visual layout once cascade, specificity, and Obsidian's own rules are in the mix.
233	
234	2. **Screenshots caught real bugs — but the screenshot fixtures were never adversarial
235	   enough, and were checked for the *specific* thing being fixed, not for regressions
236	   elsewhere.** Each round screenshotted a fixture built to demonstrate the fix just
237	   made, not a fixed, growing regression corpus covering every previously-verified
238	   scenario. A fix for fold-indicator clearance was checked against fold-indicator
239	   clearance; it was not cross-checked against the earlier "does text stay readable"
240	   scenario, so the text-overlap regression it introduced wasn't caught until a
241	   dedicated screenshot of that specific case.
242	
243	3. **Marker *size* was never scrutinized, only marker *presence/shape/position*.** Every
244	   screenshot taken in this session had headings and paragraphs with visibly different
245	   marker sizes sitting right next to each other — the data needed to catch the em/rem
246	   marker bug was in nearly every screenshot from attempt 3 onward. It wasn't caught
247	   because the reviewer (this session) was looking for "is there a marker, is it round,
248	   is it positioned correctly," not "is it the same size everywhere."
249	
250	4. **The build pipeline was a repeat source of false negatives.** `npm run build`
251	   (`tsc --noEmit`) does not rebuild `main.js`; only `npm run build:plugin` (esbuild)
252	   does. At least twice in this session, CSS/behavior was "verified" against a stale
253	   bundle, producing a confident but wrong read of the state (most dramatically: an
254	   entire verification round concluding "list items get zero decoration" when the real
255	   bug was simply that the test was running old code).
256	
257	5. **The sandboxed e2e vault and hand-built synthetic fixtures were not the real
258	   vault.** Real bugs (styles.css not loading at all; the fold-indicator collision,
259	   which only appears on list items with children; the marker-size bug, which only
260	   shows up when a heading and a paragraph are visible together) either didn't
261	   reproduce in the synthetic fixtures at all, or reproduced but weren't looked for.
262	   Testing exclusively against hand-picked, already-passing-shaped fixtures — rather
263	   than the user's actual notes, or a fixture corpus deliberately designed to be
264	   adversarial — kept validating the wrong thing.
265	
266	6. **A fully green automated test suite (198 unit + 33 e2e) was maintained by updating
267	   the tests to match whatever the current implementation did**, rather than encoding
268	   independent, fixed visual-correctness invariants that would fail if the
269	   implementation regressed. This means "all tests pass" never actually functioned as
270	   the safety net it appeared to be — it confirmed the code was internally consistent
271	   with itself, not that it was correct.
272	
273	## Carried-forward technical findings (implementation-agnostic)
274	
275	These hold regardless of what mechanism the next attempt uses:
276	
277	- `padding-left` moves content, never an element's own border/background box; anything
278	  with visible chrome (code fences, tables, callouts, quotes) needs `margin-left` or an
279	  equivalent box-moving property instead.
280	- Obsidian's native list hanging-indent is a `text-indent`(negative)+`padding-left`
281	  *pair*; if either half is overridden, both must be, together, deliberately, or the
282	  hang math breaks in ways that look like arbitrary misalignment.
283	- CSS custom properties resolve `em`/relative units at the point of *use*. Any rule
284	  meant to produce a *uniform* value across elements with different font-sizes (headings
285	  vs. body text) needs a `rem` (root-relative) or otherwise absolute unit — for every
286	  property that needs to be uniform, not just the one that was noticed first (this
287	  session fixed it for indentation and missed it for marker size in the same
288	  stylesheet).
289	- Obsidian's own `.cm-line` styles need higher specificity and often `!important` to
290	  override at all — confirmed repeatedly, not a one-off.
291	- The fold-indicator (collapse chevron) is present on any list item with children, is
292	  positioned at the same anchor as adjacent inline content, and extends a fixed pixel
293	  amount past that anchor independent of any custom indent scheme — a real collision
294	  hazard for any custom marker/overlay positioned near a list item's start.
295	- `obsidian-outliner`'s proven approach for guide lines is DOM measurement
296	  (`coordsAtPos`/`lineBlockAt`) plus absolutely-positioned overlay elements, not CSS
297	  padding/pseudo-element tricks — this session never actually implemented that approach
298	  despite researching it, and went with a CSS-only strategy throughout attempt 3, which
299	  is arguably the root cause of most of the fragility described above.
300	- This project's own prior research (`02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md`) already flagged
301	  the risk of a CSS-override-heavy strategy (the workflowy-style-outline case study,
302	  ~890 `!important` uses) before this change was ever proposed. That warning was
303	  available and was not weighed heavily enough before committing to the CSS-override
304	  strategy this session used throughout.
305	
306	## Open questions for the next iteration
307	
308	- Should guide lines and markers be implemented via DOM measurement + overlay elements
309	  (obsidian-outliner's proven pattern) instead of CSS padding/margin/text-indent
310	  overrides? This session never actually tried that path for anything beyond bullet
311	  glyph restyling.
312	- Is visual unification across encodings (headings/lists/paragraphs all reading as one
313	  indentation language) achievable at all without either (a) fully replacing native
314	  rendering for every kind — the path this project's own research already flagged as
315	  scorecard-risky — or (b) a measurement-based approach robust enough to survive
316	  arbitrary themes and fold-indicator-style native chrome this session didn't fully
317	  catalog?
318	- Is there a fundamentally different way to signal "this is one tree" that doesn't
319	  require in-place chrome matching at all — e.g., a structural overview pane, fold/zoom
320	  affordances, or something else entirely?
321	- What would a *real* verification protocol look like for this class of change — one
322	  that doesn't repeat the false-confidence patterns above? At minimum: screenshot a
323	  fixed, growing regression corpus (not just the fixture for the current fix) on every
324	  change; explicitly check *size* and *color* consistency across kinds, not just
325	  presence/position; test against the actual dev vault, not only synthetic fixtures;
326	  and treat a fully green test suite as necessary but not sufficient.
327	
328	## Current repository state
329	
330	The working tree has the full (broken) implementation uncommitted: `src/plugin/
331	decorate.ts`, `src/plugin/decorations.ts`, `styles.css`, the `main.ts` wiring, the e2e
332	spec, and updated OpenSpec artifacts for the `outline-decorations` change. Nothing has
333	been reverted — that decision (keep as a reference/starting point vs. discard) is left
334	for the next-iteration planning this document is meant to feed.
335

1	# Decoration experiments: plan (2026-07-13)
2	
3	Answers the postmortem's open question — is cross-kind visual unification achievable, and
4	how — by running small, isolated, falsifiable prototypes instead of another multi-attempt
5	scramble. See [06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md](06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md)
6	for what failed and why, and the git history of this doc's own introduction for the
7	external research that shaped this plan (obsidian-outliner's measure-don't-fight technique,
8	Logseq's per-block-component architecture, Silverbullet's declined-to-unify precedent).
9	
10	## Ground rules for every experiment below
11	
12	1. **One technique per experiment, isolated.** Each gets its own branch off a clean
13	   baseline (outline mode + grammar + structural ops, zero decoration code) so results are
14	   attributable and comparable, not entangled the way attempt 1→2→3 was last time.
15	2. **A fixed, shared fixture corpus, screenshotted every time — not just the fixture for
16	   the thing being fixed.** Every experiment runs against the *same* corpus (below), in
17	   both bundled light and dark themes. Growing the corpus is fine; shrinking scope of what
18	   gets screenshotted is not.
19	3. **A real-vault pass is mandatory before an experiment is called done**, not just the
20	   synthetic corpus. This was the single biggest false-confidence source last time.
21	4. **Record results in the comparison table at the bottom before moving to the next
22	   experiment.** Verdict is one of: keep, reject, or needs-follow-up — with the concrete
23	   reason.
24	5. **No experiment is "done" on green tests alone.** Unit tests here can only prove the
25	   pure computation is internally consistent; they cannot prove anything about rendering.
26	   Screenshot + real-vault verification is the actual gate.
27	
28	## The fixture corpus (shared across all experiments)
29	
30	- **Flat**: 3 top-level paragraphs, no lists or headings at all — the original motivating
31	  bug (`04-open-questions.md`: "with no bullets/indent chrome, outline mode is hard to
32	  verify by eye in flat documents").
33	- **Mixed**: heading → heading → nested bullet list → paragraph-as-sibling-of-list-item →
34	  code fence, matching `MIXED_MD` in the existing e2e spec.
35	- **Checkbox/task**: a top-level paragraph followed by a checkbox list, mixed
36	  checked/unchecked — the exact shape that broke in the field last time.
37	- **Heading-then-list, no paragraph**: a list directly under a heading with *no*
38	  intervening paragraph — isolates the "list item's native indentation doesn't know about
39	  heading ancestors" case, the crux of the additive-margin hypothesis.
40	- **Multi-line continuation**: a paragraph and a list item each spanning 2+ lines via
41	  `Shift+Enter` — never actually screenshotted last time; explicitly in scope now.
42	- **Wide numbering**: an ordered list crossing a digit-width boundary (items 9 and 10) —
43	  checks whether any supplemental indentation clashes with native marker-width variance.
44	- **Deep nesting**: 4+ levels mixing bullet and ordered markers.
45	
46	Committed once as [e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts](../../e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts) —
47	exported markdown-string constants (matching the project's existing `createNote(path,
48	content)` convention, not on-disk `.md` files) plus an `ALL_DECORATION_FIXTURES` array for
49	"screenshot everything" loops. Every experiment branch imports from here; don't fork or
50	re-type fixtures per experiment, or results stop being comparable.
51	
52	---
53	
54	## Experiment 1 (primary): additive-only indentation, no synthetic marker
55	
56	**Hypothesis**: your proposed compromise — drop the marker for non-list kinds entirely,
57	keep list items' native marker completely untouched, and get indentation right by *adding*
58	to what's there rather than *replacing* it.
59	
60	**Mechanism**:
61	- Paragraphs/headings/atoms: `padding-left` (atoms: `margin-left`) = `depth × unit`. Purely
62	  additive — nothing native to fight here, this part already worked in the D9 walk-back.
63	- List items: **never touch `text-indent`/`padding-left`.** Compute a new quantity —
64	  *supplemental depth* = total tree depth − depth-within-nearest-list-root (i.e. how many
65	  non-list-item ancestors sit above the nearest list root) — and apply `margin-left` =
66	  `supplemental-depth × unit` on top of native rendering. A list with no heading/paragraph
67	  ancestors above its root gets supplemental depth 0 → byte-identical to
68	  outline-mode-off, which becomes a permanent regression invariant, not a one-time check.
69	
70	**New code needed**: `decorate.ts`'s walk already computes total depth; add a second
71	counter that resets to 0 whenever a list-item ancestor chain starts, to get
72	depth-within-nearest-list-root. Keep the existing pure module's shape (no CM6 imports);
73	this is a pure-function change, unit-testable the same way the current tests are.
74	
75	**Success criteria**:
76	- Every fixture's non-list lines render additively (no property replaced, only added).
77	- Heading-then-list fixture: the list visibly shifts right by the heading's depth
78	  contribution, while its own internal per-level spacing is pixel-identical to
79	  outline-mode-off.
80	- Wide-numbering fixture: no overlap/misalignment between marker and text.
81	- Multi-line continuation: continuation lines indent identically to their node's first
82	  line, in both directions (paragraph and list item).
83	
84	**What would kill it**: if `margin-left` on a list item interacts badly with anything
85	Obsidian's own list CSS assumes about the box it's positioned in (e.g. the fold-indicator's
86	absolute positioning might be relative to a box `margin-left` moves but `padding-left`
87	wouldn't have) — check the fold-indicator specifically, since it's the one native element
88	that already burned an attempt this cycle.
89	
90	## Experiment 2 (primary): guide lines — two implementations, head to head
91	
92	Build **both** below against the same corpus and compare; don't pick a favorite in advance.
93	
94	**2a — overlay-measured (obsidian-outliner's proven technique).** A `ViewPlugin` reads
95	already-rendered pixel positions via `view.coordsAtPos()`/`view.lineBlockAt()` *after*
96	Experiment 1's indentation has been laid out, and draws absolutely-positioned overlay
97	`<div>`s (inline-styled from JS, not CSS classes) in a layer outside `.cm-content`.
98	Recompute debounced on `docChanged || viewportChanged`, not every `ViewUpdate` (a real
99	perf/correctness improvement over this cycle's D4 choice, now that the mode-toggle-poke
100	problem can be solved with a dedicated `StateEffect` instead of an empty dispatch forcing a
101	full recompute path every time).
102	
103	**2b — CSS stacked-gradient (the `@replit/codemirror-indentation-markers` technique).** A
104	single `Decoration.line` per line sets one CSS custom property to a comma-joined list of
105	`repeating-linear-gradient(...)` layers (one per ancestor level), consumed by a single
106	`::before` — O(1) DOM nodes per line regardless of depth, no JS pixel measurement at all.
107	
108	**Compare on**: correctness against the multi-line continuation fixture specifically (2a
109	gets wrapped-row height for free via `lineBlockAt`; 2b needs the line's own rendered height,
110	which should also be free since it's one element per whole line — verify this isn't
111	secretly harder); resilience across light/dark theme and a couple of community themes if
112	convenient; code size/complexity; whether 2b's reliance on the cascade re-triggers *any* of
113	the `!important`/specificity problems from last time (it shouldn't, since guides are a new
114	property no native rule contests — but verify, don't assume).
115	
116	**Success criteria**: guide lines visibly connect a node to its ancestors' guides at every
117	fixture's nesting, including the multi-line continuation case (never verified last time).
118	
119	## Experiment 3 (conditional — only run if Experiment 1's flat fixture fails review)
120	
121	**Trigger**: if dropping the marker means the **flat** fixture (3 top-level paragraphs, all
122	at depth 0, so indentation alone conveys nothing) still doesn't read as "outline mode is
123	on" — which is the *original* motivating bug this whole change exists to fix — try a
124	minimal, low-risk marker that avoids the em/rem bug class *by construction*: a fixed-px-
125	width left border or background tint (not a `::before` glyph with a font-size), so there's
126	no font-size context to inherit incorrectly regardless of which kind's line it's on.
127	
128	**Success criteria**: flat fixture reads as distinct nodes at a glance, in a screenshot,
129	without reintroducing any font-size-dependent sizing.
130	
131	**If this also isn't good enough**: fall back to the overlay-measured marker (same
132	mechanism as 2a, sized as a fixed absolute value, never inheriting font-size by
133	construction) — this is the "real" fix for the original marker-size bug, just held in
134	reserve since it's more implementation work than the compromise you proposed.
135	
136	## Experiment 4 (optional risk-spike — only if Experiment 1 still feels fragile on real content)
137	
138	Spike the `Decoration.widget` spacer technique (real inline content pushing text via layout
139	width, instead of any CSS box-model property) in isolation, specifically against the
140	multi-line/wrapped-continuation fixture, to test the one confirmed real gotcha found in
141	research: CM6 wraps every widget in an invisible `.cm-widgetBuffer` that can introduce
142	stray line-wrap points. Go in expecting this might kill the idea — it's unproven in the
143	wild for exactly this reason, and the whole CM6 community converged on CSS `Decoration.line`
144	instead. Only worth spending time on if Experiment 1's `padding-left`/`margin-left`
145	approach keeps showing cascade fragility against real themes despite the additive-only
146	discipline.
147	
148	## Track 5 (parallel infrastructure, not gated — apply to all of the above)
149	
150	- Commit the fixture corpus once; every experiment's verification re-screenshots all of it,
151	  not just its own new fixture.
152	- One automated check: render two elements of deliberately different font-size (a heading
153	  line and a paragraph line) at the same computed tree depth, diff *every*
154	  decoration-related computed style at once (`padding-left`, `margin-left`, any marker
155	  `font-size`/`width`/`height`). This single mechanism would have caught both the
156	  indentation em/rem bug and the marker-size em/rem bug last time, instead of two separate
157	  manual catches.
158	- Fix `npm run build` to actually invoke esbuild, or add an explicit pre-verification step
159	  (`npm run build:plugin && npm run vault:install`) to the checklist so a stale bundle
160	  can't produce a false "verified" result again.
161	- A real dev-vault pass is part of *every* experiment's exit criteria, not a final step
162	  after all experiments conclude.
163	
164	## Architecture question: per-block editor tree — resolved, not prototyped
165	
166	Researched via your `continuous-journal` plugin as the concrete test case (see chat).
167	Verdict: not a lighter alternative to the already-rejected custom-view path — it requires a
168	comparable-or-larger private-API footprint (leaf-grafting, `setActiveLeaf` monkey-patching)
169	plus a genuinely unsolved new problem (N editors bound to overlapping ranges of one shared
170	file, kept consistent as any one of them edits). Shelved against this project's decided
171	100%-public-API bar (Q1); revisit only if that bar itself is ever renegotiated.

174	
175	Quick-scan status; full detail in the subsections below.
176	
177	| # | Technique | Status | Verdict |
178	|---|---|---|---|
179	| 1 | Additive indentation, no marker | Done, three real bugs found and fixed | **Keep** |
180	| 2a | Guides — overlay-measured | Done, three real bugs found and fixed | **Keep** |

184	
185	### Experiment 1 — additive indentation, no marker
186	
187	**Verdict: keep.** All 8 corpus fixtures pass, both themes, screenshotted and visually
188	reviewed (not just DOM-asserted) — see
189	[e2e/specs/50-decorations.e2e.ts](../../e2e/specs/50-decorations.e2e.ts). The user toured
190	every note in the real `test-vault` by hand — the actual dev-vault pass the postmortem
191	insisted on, not a formality — and found three real bugs the synthetic corpus missed
192	entirely (below). All three are fixed, with permanent fixtures/assertions added so they
193	can't silently regress. User's assessment after the fixes: "much more promising" than the
194	prior attempt — indentation alone gives real hierarchy legibility switching modes on and
195	off, more than initially expected going in.
196	
197	**Code cost**: ~15 lines added to `decorate.ts` (2 new fields + 1 extra walk parameter),
198	~150-line `decorations.ts` adapter (a `StateField` for plain lines plus a `ViewPlugin` for
199	widget-replaced atoms), ~25-line `styles.css`.
200	
201	**Bugs found and fixed** (chronological — each was caught only by a human looking at real,
202	organic content, never by the synthetic corpus or DOM assertions):
203	
204	1. **Cascade fight, caught in the synthetic corpus.** Obsidian's own `app.css` has
205	   `.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > * { margin: 0px !important; }` — three
206	   classes of specificity, which beat our original two-class `.cm-line.to-decor-*` selector
207	   outright even with `!important` on both sides. Fixed by matching Obsidian's own
208	   ancestor-chain specificity, not by escalating `!important` further (there's nowhere
209	   further to escalate — the tie-break past equal `!important` is specificity, then source
210	   order).
211	2. **Tables and callouts not indenting at all, caught by the user in the real vault.**
212	   Obsidian renders tables, callouts, raw HTML blocks, and (surprisingly) horizontal rules
213	   as opaque replacement widgets in Live Preview (`.cm-embed-block`, or `.hr` for the rule,
214	   which oddly still carries a `cm-line` class despite being widget-rendered). A
215	   `Decoration.line` targeting that source line has **no effect at all** — not even a
216	   partial class-merge win, confirmed live by dumping the DOM: both class and inline style
217	   come back completely empty. Code fences and plain blockquotes are genuinely plain
218	   `.cm-line`s and were unaffected, which is exactly why the synthetic corpus (which only
219	   exercised those two atom kinds) never caught this. Fixed with a companion `ViewPlugin`
220	   that finds these widgets directly after each render (`docViewUpdate`, not the pre-render
221	   `update()` hook — DOM reads/writes need the widget to already exist) and sets
222	   `margin-left` inline with `!important`, which always wins over *any* stylesheet rule
223	   regardless of specificity — a clean escape hatch for exactly this class of native-widget
224	   cascade fight. It reads the same `--to-decor-unit` CSS variable the class-based rules
225	   use, so the unit constant stays single-sourced.
226	3. **Table still visibly offset from code/callout after fix #2, caught by the user.** Once
227	   tables got a margin at all, they still sat slightly further right than a same-depth code
228	   block or callout. Root cause: `.cm-embed-block.cm-table-widget` carries its own native
229	   left padding (for the row/column drag-handle icons) that callout's wrapper doesn't have.
230	   Padding never moves an element's own box — invisible for code/callout, whose *background*
231	   fills their whole padded box — but the table's actual visible content (a `<table>` nested
232	   two levels inside the padded wrapper) sits in normal flow within that padding, so it
233	   visually renders offset by exactly that amount. Fixed by reading the widget's own
234	   `getComputedStyle(...).paddingLeft` live (not a hardcoded pixel constant, which would be
235	   theme/version-fragile) and subtracting it from the applied margin, clamped at zero so a
236	   depth-0 atom never goes negative.
237	
238	**Deferred, not fixed on this branch**: list items sit visually further right than a
239	sibling paragraph/blockquote at the same tree depth. Traced to a real file
240	(`Kitchen Renovation.md`) and confirmed via measurement that our own added margin is
241	*identical* across all three at that depth — the extra offset is Obsidian's own native
242	list-item hang (`text-indent: -Npx` / `padding-left: Npx`), which already pushes list text
243	right of a sibling paragraph's text in **vanilla Obsidian, mode on or off**. It's not
244	something this experiment introduced; it's just more noticeable now that everything else
245	lines up precisely. Fixing it the way `obsidian-outliner`'s `BetterListsStyles` does means
246	touching that native `text-indent`/`padding-left` pair directly — the single riskiest
247	surface the postmortem blamed for most of the prior failed attempt's bugs. Left alone
248	pending a deliberate, isolated follow-up; see
249	[open question](#open-question-shrinking-only-our-own-added-list-margin) below for a
250	narrower variant that stays additive-only.
251	
252	Also confirmed **not** a bug: the flat fixture (3 top-level paragraphs, all depth 0) shows
253	no visual change from outline-mode-off. This was flagged going in as Experiment 3's
254	trigger condition, but the user confirmed on real content that this reads correctly the
255	moment a heading sits above the paragraphs — depth-0-with-no-ancestor genuinely has nothing
256	to convey, which is correct, not a legibility gap.
257	
258	### Experiment 2a — guide lines via pixel-measured overlay
259	
260	**Verdict: keep.** Branched off Experiment 1 (`experiment/decorations-2a-guides-overlay`).
261	All 8 corpus fixtures plus 4 real vault notes pass, both themes, screenshotted and visually
262	reviewed at pixel level (not just DOM-asserted) — see
263	[e2e/specs/51-guides-overlay.e2e.ts](../../e2e/specs/51-guides-overlay.e2e.ts), 43/43 e2e
264	tests green across the full suite (not just this spec). One ancestor-with-children gets one
265	continuous overlay `<div>` spanning from its first child's own line to its deepest
266	descendant's last line, positioned by *measuring* (`view.coordsAtPos`/`view.lineBlockAt`)
267	the ancestor's own already-rendered position rather than recomputing depth × unit —
268	obsidian-outliner's technique, applied to this project's universal (not list-only) tree.
269	
270	**Real-vault finding, fixed on this branch (not a separate follow-up)**: a *list-item*
271	ancestor gets no guide of its own, deliberately. The user's own real-vault comparison
272	(built-in indent guides only / both / ours only, screenshotted side by side) showed two
273	problems, both traced to the same cause: (a) with Obsidian's native "Show indent guides"
274	setting on, list nesting got visibly doubled lines; (b) even alone, our guides within a
275	list read as unevenly spaced, because native list nesting has its own internal per-level
276	width that our fixed unit doesn't match (the same native-hang mismatch Experiment 1 already
277	deferred rather than fight, now visible one layer up). Native indent guides already connect
278	one bullet precisely to the next *within* a list — there is no gap for a second mechanism to
279	usefully fill there. Fixed by having `computeGuides` skip pushing a guide when the anchor
280	node's `kind === 'list-item'`, while still including list-item subtrees in a *non-list*
281	ancestor's own span (a heading or paragraph bridging into a list still gets its guide,
282	reaching all the way through — only the within-list levels get none of their own). This
283	turns, e.g., the `deep-nesting` fixture (four levels, all list items) into zero guides at
284	all — entirely deferred to native — while `heading-then-list` keeps exactly one guide
285	(the heading's, spanning the whole list) instead of three.
286	
287	**Deliberate deviation from the plan's literal wording**: the plan called for "a dedicated
288	`StateEffect`" for the out-of-band mode-toggle refresh. Dispatching a raw CM6 effect from
289	`main.ts` (application code, not a registered extension) has no path through Obsidian's
290	public `Editor` API — `EditorTransaction` exposes no `effects` field, and reaching into
291	`(editor as any).cm.dispatch()` is exactly the private-API surface this project's own
292	research already flags to avoid outside registered extensions
293	([03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md](03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md)). Used per-instance state
294	comparison instead (`ViewPlugin`/`StateField` each remember the outline-mode flag they last
295	saw and compare on every transaction — cheap, since it's just a boolean read, not a
296	reparse): functionally identical gate (`docChanged || viewportChanged || mode-just-toggled`),
297	zero private API. `main.ts`'s existing cursor-nudge dispatch (already public-API, unchanged)
298	still supplies the transaction that gives the comparison a chance to run.
299	
300	**Code cost**: ~35 lines added to `decorate.ts` (`computeGuides`, a second tree walk),
301	~140-line addition to `decorations.ts` (a hand-rolled, debounced `ViewPlugin` reusing
302	`@codemirror/view`'s own exported `RectangleMarker` as a positioned-div factory, not CM6's
303	higher-level `layer()` helper — see the in-code rationale for why), ~20-line `styles.css`.
304	
305	**Bugs found and fixed** (all three caught only by real e2e rendering/measurement — the
306	*pure* `computeGuides` unit tests were correct throughout; every bug was in the CM6
307	coordinate-system plumbing, exactly the kind of thing the postmortem warned unit tests
308	can't see):
309	
310	1. **Guides rendered ~76px too high, caught by an e2e rect assertion, root-caused via live
311	   DOM diagnostics.** `view.lineBlockAt(pos).top/.bottom` are relative to `view.documentTop`
312	   (itself viewport-relative, moves with scroll) — not to the guide layer's own container
313	   origin (`scrollDOM`'s top-left, where the container's `top: 0; left: 0` anchors it). The
314	   two differ by a real, non-zero, confirmed-live constant (`.cm-content`'s own offset within
315	   the scroller). Fixed by converting through `documentTop` the same way `coordsAtPos`'s
316	   result already gets converted through a `getBase()`-style subtraction for the x-axis —
317	   the asymmetry (only x was being converted, not y) is what let this slip through code
318	   review.
319	2. **A list item's guide anchored ~12px right of its own bullet, caught by an e2e rect
320	   assertion against `.cm-formatting-list`.** `coordsAtPos(pos, 1)` ("after"-biased) at a
321	   line's very first position returns the *far* edge of the first character — past the
322	   bullet glyph, not at its start. `coordsAtPos(pos, -1)` ("before"-biased) gives the
323	   character's own left edge, matching the bullet's true rendered start.
324	3. **A doubly-nested list item's guide anchored a full level too far left, caught by the
325	   same assertion at a deeper fixture level.** A nested list item's raw source line *starts
326	   with its own indentation whitespace* (`"  - nested item"`); Obsidian visually collapses
327	   that leading whitespace via its native indent mechanism rather than rendering it at
328	   normal character width, so `coordsAtPos` at the raw line's first position (a space)
329	   lands at the *parent* list level's column, not the marker's. Fixed by skipping past
330	   `text.length - text.trimStart().length` characters before measuring — general, not
331	   list-item-specific (a no-op for headings/paragraphs, which have no leading whitespace).
332	
333	All three were "invisible" in a full-page screenshot glance (subtle 1px, low-opacity lines)
334	and only surfaced by comparing exact measured coordinates — directly reinforcing the
335	postmortem's central lesson (DOM assertions test that code ran; here, pixel-level rect
336	assertions were what actually caught bugs a screenshot glance and a passing unit-test suite
337	both missed). A closer crop of one screenshot region did independently confirm the fix
338	visually once the coordinates were right.
339	
340	**Multi-line continuation (the case never even attempted last time)**: confirmed via e2e
341	that `lineBlockAt`'s block-level `.top`/`.bottom` correctly span a Shift+Enter-continued
342	node's full rendered height "for free," for both a paragraph and a list item — no special
343	handling needed once the coordinate-conversion bugs above were fixed.
344

364	### Open question: shrinking only our own added list margin
365	
366	Raised by the user, not yet decided. The deferred list-hang issue (above) is native
367	Obsidian chrome and explicitly out of scope for direct edits in this experiment. But a
368	narrower variant stays inside the additive-only discipline: **reduce only the margin *we*
369	add** to list items — not native `text-indent`/`padding-left` — by the list's own native
370	hang width, read live via `getComputedStyle` the same way the table fix (bug #3 above)
371	reads and compensates for native padding. Worth exploring as a follow-up, with two open
372	risks to resolve before trying it: (a) clamping so a shallow `supplementalDepth` never goes
373	negative once the hang is subtracted (the table fix's `max(0px, ...)` pattern applies
374	directly), and (b) the compensation must be based on the list **root**'s own hang, not each
375	item's — nested items can have wider markers (e.g. `10.` vs `-`) with different native hang
376	widths, and compensating per-item instead of per-root-chain would reintroduce exactly the
377	kind of within-list misalignment the wide-numbering fixture exists to catch.
378	
379	## Non-obvious findings
380	
381	Cross-experiment learnings, carried forward the same way the postmortem's own "carried-forward
382	technical findings" section was meant to be used — read this before starting 2a/2b/3/4:
383	
384	- **CM6 `Decoration.line` has zero effect on Obsidian's "embed-block" replacement widgets**
385	  (tables, callouts, raw HTML, and horizontal rules) — not a partial win, not a class-merge;
386	  confirmed live that both class and inline style come back completely empty. Any atom kind
387	  Obsidian renders this way needs direct DOM patching via a `ViewPlugin`'s `docViewUpdate`
388	  hook, not a CM6 decoration. Code fences and plain blockquotes are *not* in this category —
389	  they render as genuinely plain `.cm-line`s and decorate normally.
390	- **A single broad selector plus a fact lookup by document line number is enough** to handle
391	  all four widget-replaced kinds uniformly (`.cm-embed-block, .cm-line.hr` → `posAtDOM` →
392	  `decorate()` facts by line) — no need for kind-specific branches.
393	- **Matching beats escalating.** When Obsidian's own CSS wins a specificity fight even
394	  against `!important`, the fix is matching its ancestor-chain specificity, not adding more
395	  `!important` (there's nowhere further to escalate past equal importance — the tie-break is
396	  specificity, then source order).
397	- **An inline style with `!important`, set via JS, always wins over any stylesheet rule**
398	  regardless of that rule's own specificity — a reliable escape hatch specifically for
399	  native-widget cascade fights that a CSS-only rule can't win.
400	- **Padding's visibility depends on where the background lives.** Padding never moves an
401	  element's own box (carried forward from the original postmortem), but whether that's
402	  visually invisible or visually offsetting depends on whether the element's own background
403	  fills the padded box (invisible, as for callout) or the visible content is nested one level
404	  further in without its own covering background (offsetting, as for a `<table>` inside a
405	  padded wrapper).
406	- **Read native values live instead of hardcoding compensations.** Subtracting a
407	  `getComputedStyle(...)`-read native padding from an own-added margin (clamped at zero)
408	  stays correct across themes/Obsidian versions; a hardcoded pixel constant would silently
409	  drift the moment a theme changes that padding.
410	- **List items already sit right of sibling text in vanilla Obsidian** — the native
411	  `text-indent`/`padding-left` hang pair reserves room for the bullet regardless of outline
412	  mode. This experiment's additive design correctly preserved that native behavior
413	  (verified: our own added margin is identical across list/paragraph/blockquote at the same
414	  depth); the extra offset users notice is pre-existing Obsidian behavior becoming more
415	  visible now that everything else aligns precisely, not a regression this experiment
416	  introduced.
417	- **The synthetic corpus, even a deliberately adversarial one, missed all three real bugs
418	  above.** Every one was caught only by a human visually reviewing real, organic vault
419	  content (not synthetic fixtures, not DOM-attribute assertions). This reaffirms the
420	  postmortem's central lesson directly, in a new implementation: treat the real-vault pass
421	  as load-bearing verification for every future experiment, not a final formality after the
422	  fixtures pass.
423	- **CM6's coordinate systems are not interchangeable, and mixing them silently compiles.**
424	  `coordsAtPos()` is viewport-relative (like `getBoundingClientRect`); `lineBlockAt()`'s
425	  `top`/`bottom` are relative to `view.documentTop` instead — a *different* origin, even
426	  though both "sound" document-relative. Converting only one axis (x, via a `getBase()`-
427	  style subtraction) and not the other (y, left as the raw `lineBlockAt` value) type-checks
428	  fine and looks plausible in code review; it produced a ~76px vertical offset only caught
429	  by an e2e rect assertion against independently-measured `.cm-line` rects, never by the
430	  pure-function unit tests (which never touch real coordinates at all). Any experiment doing
431	  its own pixel measurement (2b's gradient positioning included, if it ends up needing any)
432	  should audit *every* axis's reference frame explicitly, not assume symmetry.
433	- **`coordsAtPos(pos, side)`'s side parameter matters at line starts, and defaults are not
434	  obviously safe.** `side: 1` ("after"-biased) at a line's very first position returns the
435	  far edge of the first character — for a list item, that's past the bullet glyph, not at
436	  its start. `side: -1` ("before"-biased) gives the character's own left edge. Untested,
437	  `side: 1` compiles and "looks right" (it did return *a* plausible-looking coordinate) —
438	  only a rect comparison against the actual rendered marker (`.cm-formatting-list`) caught
439	  the ~12px discrepancy.
440	- **A nested list item's raw source line starts with its own indentation whitespace, and
441	  Obsidian doesn't render that whitespace at literal character width.** Measuring
442	  `coordsAtPos` at a line's raw `.from` (before skipping leading whitespace) lands at
443	  wherever Obsidian's own indent mechanism collapses that whitespace to — which turned out
444	  to be the *parent* list level's own column, not the current line's marker. Any code
445	  measuring "where does this line's real content start" needs to skip
446	  `text.length - text.trimStart().length` characters first; this is general (headings/
447	  paragraphs have no leading whitespace, so it's a no-op there), not a list-item special case.
448	- **CM6 ships a first-party `layer()`/`RectangleMarker` pair (`@codemirror/view`, used
449	  internally for the selection/cursor layers) purpose-built for "measured overlay outside
450	  `.cm-content`, scrolls naturally."** `RectangleMarker` alone (just the positioned-div
451	  factory, not the full `layer()` wrapper) was reused here since `layer()`'s own `update()`
452	  hook only supports a synchronous redraw with no way to thread in the plan's requested
453	  debounce — but the full `layer()` extension is worth trying as-is for 2b or a future
454	  revisit if debouncing turns out not to matter in practice; it would remove the need for
455	  this experiment's manual scroll-coordinate calibration entirely.
456	- **A custom guide mechanism must actively coexist with Obsidian's own native "Show indent
457	  guides" setting for lists, not just avoid crashing next to it.** Real-vault side-by-side
458	  comparison (native only / both / ours only) showed native list guides are already precise
459	  (bullet to bullet) in a way a block-level, fixed-unit overlay can't match — running both
460	  at once visibly doubles lines; ours alone (if drawn for list nesting) reads as unevenly
461	  spaced. The fix wasn't CSS suppression of the native feature (obsidian-outliner's own
462	  approach, per the original postmortem) but scope reduction: never draw our guide where
463	  the *native* mechanism already owns the job (any list-item ancestor), only where native
464	  has zero representation at all (bridging a non-list ancestor into a list, or between
465	  non-list kinds). **2b will face this identical question** — a CSS-gradient guide is
466	  exactly as capable of doubling against native list guides as an overlay div is; the same
467	  list-item-ancestor exclusion should apply there too, not be treated as an 2a-specific fix.
468	
469	## Setup (done, 2026-07-13)
470	
471	- Full attempt-3 snapshot (broken code + the docs as they stood) preserved on
472	  `archive/outline-decorations-attempt-3` — reference only, do not build on it.
473	- `feat/outline-decorations` reset to a clean baseline: `decorate.ts` + its unit tests kept
474	  (never the buggy layer), `decorations.ts`/`styles.css`/the old e2e spec dropped (rebuild
475	  per experiment, not patched in place), `main.ts` reverted to no decoration wiring,
476	  `design.md`/`spec.md`/`tasks.md` annotated paused-with-context rather than left describing
477	  a scope no code matches, package.json/.gitignore's `styles.css`-symlink fix kept, shared
478	  fixture corpus committed at `e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts`.
479	- `experiment/decorations-1-additive-indent` branched off the clean baseline, ready to start.
480	- `experiment/decorations-2a-*`/`2b-*` branch off **1**, not off the clean baseline, once it
481	  validates — guides need some indentation already rendered to meaningfully measure/draw
482	  against. Create with `git checkout experiment/decorations-1-additive-indent && git
483	  checkout -b experiment/decorations-2a-guides-overlay` (and `-2b-guides-css-gradient`
484	  similarly) once 1 is done.
485	- Experiments 3 and 4 are conditional/optional — branch only if triggered (see below).
486	
487	## Handoff prompts
488	
489	Self-contained prompts, one per experiment — paste into a fresh session (or hand to a
490	subagent) to start that experiment in isolation. Each assumes zero prior context beyond
491	what's inline plus the two linked docs.
492	
493	### Experiment 1 — additive-only indentation, no marker
494	
495	> Branch: `experiment/decorations-1-additive-indent` (already created off a clean
496	> `feat/outline-decorations`, no decoration code present — `decorate.ts` and its tests are
497	> the only survivors from a prior failed attempt, kept because they were never the buggy
498	> layer). Read `docs/research/06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md` and
499	> `docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md` (Experiment 1's section) for full
500	> context before starting.
501	>
502	> Build: paragraphs/headings/atoms get plain `padding-left`/`margin-left` = `depth × unit`
503	> (a fixed rem/px constant, never `em` — the postmortem's marker-size bug was exactly an
504	> `em` resolving against the wrong element's font-size; don't repeat that class of bug for
505	> *any* property here). List items must **never** have `text-indent`/`padding-left`
506	> touched — instead add a NEW computed quantity, supplemental depth = total tree depth −
507	> depth-within-nearest-list-root (i.e. how many non-list-item ancestors sit above the
508	> nearest list root), and apply only `margin-left` = `supplemental-depth × unit` on top of
509	> native rendering. No synthetic marker glyph on any kind — list items keep their fully
510	> native, untouched bullet/number.
511	>
512	> `decorate.ts` already computes total depth; add the second counter (resets to 0 whenever
513	> a list-item ancestor chain starts) as a new field on `LineDecorationFact`, extending the
514	> existing unit tests rather than replacing them. `decorations.ts`/`styles.css` get rebuilt
515	> from scratch (nothing to salvage from the archived attempt) using this additive-only
516	> discipline.
517	>
518	> Test against every fixture in `e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts` (not just the one that seems
519	> relevant) — screenshot each in both bundled light and dark themes. Specifically verify:
520	> the heading-then-list fixture shows the list shifted right by the heading's depth
521	> contribution while its own internal per-level spacing stays pixel-identical to
522	> outline-mode-off; the wide-numbering fixture shows no marker/text overlap; multi-line
523	> continuation fixtures indent their continuation lines identically to the node's first
524	> line. Explicitly check the fold-indicator (collapse chevron on list items with children)
525	> for collisions — the one native element that already burned an attempt this cycle.
526	>
527	> `npm run build` only runs `tsc --noEmit` — it does NOT rebuild `main.js`. Use `npm run
528	> build:plugin` (or `npm run vault:install`) before any real-vault check, or you'll verify
529	> a stale bundle. A real dev-vault pass (not just the synthetic fixtures) is required before
530	> calling this done. Record the result in the comparison table in
531	> `docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md`.
532	
533	### Experiment 2a — guide lines via pixel-measured overlay
534	
535	> Prerequisite: Experiment 1 validated and merged/available. Branch off it:
536	> `git checkout experiment/decorations-1-additive-indent && git checkout -b
537	> experiment/decorations-2a-guides-overlay`. Read
538	> `docs/research/06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md` and
539	> `docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md` (Experiment 2 section) first.
540	>
541	> Build a `ViewPlugin` that, after each debounced recompute (gate on `docChanged ||
542	> viewportChanged`, NOT unconditionally on every `ViewUpdate` — the prior attempt's
543	> per-ViewUpdate recompute was more expensive than needed), reads already-rendered pixel
544	> positions via `view.coordsAtPos()`/`view.lineBlockAt()` and draws one absolutely-
545	> positioned overlay `<div>` per ancestor level, inline-styled (`el.style.top/left/height`
546	> set directly from JS, not via CSS classes) into a layer living outside `.cm-content` —
547	> this is `obsidian-outliner`'s proven technique (`VerticalLines.ts` in its source), not
548	> something to reinvent from scratch; read that file if you want the exact reference
549	> shape. Handle the out-of-band mode-toggle refresh with a dedicated `StateEffect` rather
550	> than an empty `view.dispatch({})` forcing a full recompute path.
551	>
552	> Test against every fixture in `e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts`, in both themes. The specific
553	> case to get right that was never even attempted last time: the multi-line/wrapped
554	> continuation fixture — a guide line must span the full rendered height of a multi-line
555	> node (should come free from `lineBlockAt`, which is block-level and covers all wrapped
556	> visual rows — verify this rather than assuming it). `npm run build:plugin`/`vault:install`
557	> before any real-vault check. Record results in the comparison table.
558	
559	### Experiment 2b — guide lines via CSS stacked-gradient
560	
561	> Prerequisite: Experiment 1 validated and merged/available. Branch off it:
562	> `git checkout experiment/decorations-1-additive-indent && git checkout -b
563	> experiment/decorations-2b-guides-css-gradient`. Read the same two docs as 2a first — this
564	> is a deliberate head-to-head alternative to 2a, not a fallback; build both, don't assume
565	> a winner in advance.
566	>
567	> Build guide lines as a single `Decoration.line` per line, with one CSS custom property
568	> set to a comma-joined list of `repeating-linear-gradient(...)` layers (one per ancestor
569	> level), consumed by a single `::before` — this is the technique
570	> `@replit/codemirror-indentation-markers` uses (its `src/index.ts` is the reference if you
571	> want the exact shape): O(1) DOM nodes per line regardless of depth, no JS pixel
572	> measurement, no overlay layer. Because this reintroduces a real CSS rule participating in
573	> the normal cascade (unlike 2a's inline-styled overlay), explicitly verify it does NOT
574	> resurrect any of the specificity/`!important` problems from the original postmortem — it
575	> shouldn't, since guide-drawing isn't a property any native rule already contests, but
576	> confirm rather than assume.
577	>
578	> Same fixture corpus, same both-themes requirement, same multi-line-continuation focus as
579	> 2a (get the line's own rendered full height for the gradient — should be free since it's
580	> one element per whole line, but verify). Record results in the comparison table,
581	> specifically comparing code size/complexity and robustness against 2a.
582	
583	### Experiment 3 — minimal marker fallback (conditional)
584	
585	> Only start this if Experiment 1's real-vault/screenshot review of the **flat** fixture
586	> (`FLAT_MD` in `e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts`) shows indentation alone doesn't read as
587	> "outline mode is on" — everything's at depth 0, so indentation conveys nothing, which is
588	> the *original* motivating bug this whole change exists to fix
589	> (`docs/research/04-open-questions.md`: "with no bullets/indent chrome, outline mode is
590	> hard to verify by eye in flat documents"). Branch off whichever of 1/2a/2b is current
591	> baseline at the time.
592	>
593	> Build a marker that cannot inherit font-size by construction — e.g. a fixed-px-width left
594	> border or background tint on paragraph/heading/atom lines, not a `::before` glyph sized in
595	> `em`. Verify the flat fixture reads as distinct nodes at a glance in a screenshot. If this
596	> still isn't legible enough, the fallback-of-the-fallback is the overlay-measured marker
597	> (same mechanism as 2a, fixed absolute size) — more implementation work, held in reserve.
598	
599	### Experiment 4 — widget-spacer risk-spike (optional)
600	
601	> Only start this if Experiment 1's `padding-left`/`margin-left` approach keeps showing
602	> cascade fragility against real themes/content despite the additive-only discipline. Spike
603	> `Decoration.widget` (an inline spacer with real computed pixel width, pushing content via
604	> layout rather than any CSS box-model property) in isolation, specifically against the
605	> multi-line/wrapped-continuation fixture. Go in expecting it might fail: CM6 wraps every
606	> widget in an invisible `.cm-widgetBuffer` (a confirmed, maintainer-acknowledged,
607	> unresolved gotcha) that can introduce stray line-wrap points — exactly the risk for
608	> wrapped text. The whole CM6 community converged on CSS `Decoration.line` instead for this
609	> exact reason; this spike exists to find out definitively whether that gotcha actually
610	> manifests for us; do not treat this as a preferred direction going in.

1	/**
2	 * Pure per-line decoration facts for outline mode's additive-only indentation
3	 * (see docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md, Experiment 1).
4	 *
5	 * Headings, paragraphs, and atoms have no native indentation of their own —
6	 * decorations.ts sets `padding-left`/`margin-left` = `depth × unit` directly,
7	 * which is additive in effect because there is nothing native to add to.
8	 *
9	 * List items are different: Obsidian already hangs their native bullet/
10	 * number via its own `text-indent`/`padding-left` pair, per raw nesting
11	 * level. That pair is NEVER touched here — instead, `supplementalDepth`
12	 * captures only the contribution from non-list-item ancestors (e.g. a
13	 * heading a list sits under), and decorations.ts adds it as `margin-left`,
14	 * a box-model property native list rendering doesn't otherwise use. A list
15	 * with no non-list-item ancestors gets supplementalDepth 0 everywhere in it
16	 * — byte-identical to outline-mode-off, a permanent regression invariant.
17	 */
18	
19	import type { OutlineDoc, OutlineNode } from '../model';
20	import { isAtom } from '../model';
21	
22	export interface LineDecorationFact {
23	  /** 0-indexed absolute line number in the document. */
24	  readonly lineNumber: number;
25	  /** Distance from the document root; top-level nodes are depth 0. */
26	  readonly depth: number;
27	  /** True only for a node's own first line — carries the hang + marker. */
28	  readonly isFirstLine: boolean;
29	  /**
30	   * True for list-item first lines: they already have a native marker
31	   * glyph (bullet/number) that Experiment 1 leaves fully untouched — kept
32	   * for callers that need to identify it, not consumed by decorations.ts.
33	   */
34	  readonly hasNativeMarker: boolean;
35	  /**
36	   * True for atom nodes (code/table/quote/callout/html/hr). `padding-left`
37	   * only shifts an element's own *content*, never its own border/background
38	   * box — invisible for plain text, but atoms render a visible background/
39	   * border box whose edges stay put regardless of padding. Atoms need
40	   * `margin-left` instead, which actually moves the box; decorations.ts
41	   * uses this flag to pick the right CSS property.
42	   */
43	  readonly isAtom: boolean;
44	  /**
45	   * True for every line (first + continuation) of a list-item node. Native
46	   * `text-indent`/`padding-left` must never be touched for these lines —
47	   * decorations.ts applies `supplementalDepth` as `margin-left` instead,
48	   * on top of native rendering.
49	   */
50	  readonly isListItem: boolean;
51	  /**
52	   * Meaningful only when `isListItem` is true (0 and unused otherwise): the
53	   * depth, in the whole tree, of the nearest ancestor list-item that starts
54	   * an unbroken list-item chain (i.e. total tree depth minus depth-within-
55	   * that-chain) — equivalently, how many non-list-item ancestors sit above
56	   * the nearest list root. Constant across an entire nested list, so native
57	   * per-level spacing within the list is untouched; only the list's start
58	   * position shifts by its non-list ancestors' contribution.
59	   */
60	  readonly supplementalDepth: number;
61	}
62	
63	/**
64	 * Walks the parsed tree in document order (a node's own lines, then its
65	 * children — the same layout `nodeAtLine`/`startLine` assume: trailingGap
66	 * lines sit between a node's own lines and its children, and carry no fact
67	 * of their own).
68	 */
69	export function decorate(doc: OutlineDoc): LineDecorationFact[] {
70	  const facts: LineDecorationFact[] = [];
71	  let current = doc.preamble.length;
72	
73	  const walk = (node: OutlineNode, depth: number, listRootDepth: number | null): void => {
74	    const atom = isAtom(node);
75	    const isListItem = node.kind === 'list-item';
76	    // Entering a new list-item chain (this node's parent wasn't one) roots
77	    // it at this node's own depth; continuing a chain inherits the root.
78	    const rootDepth = isListItem ? (listRootDepth ?? depth) : null;
79	    for (let i = 0; i < node.lines.length; i++) {
80	      facts.push({
81	        lineNumber: current + i,
82	        depth,
83	        isFirstLine: i === 0,
84	        hasNativeMarker: isListItem && i === 0,
85	        isAtom: atom,
86	        isListItem,
87	        supplementalDepth: isListItem ? rootDepth! : 0,
88	      });
89	    }
90	    current += node.lines.length + node.trailingGap.length;
91	    node.children.forEach((child) => walk(child, depth + 1, rootDepth));
92	  };
93	
94	  doc.children.forEach((node) => walk(node, 0, null));
95	  return facts;
96	}
97

input.prompt: Extract the full source code of this file verbatim, especially how it builds the CSS custom property / repeating-linear-gradient layers for indentation markers per line, how it counts levels, and how the ::before pseudo-element CSS is written.

result.output:
# Indentation Markers Implementation Analysis

## CSS Gradient Construction

The code builds indentation markers using CSS custom properties and repeating linear gradients. The `createGradient` function generates:

```
"repeating-linear-gradient(to right, var(${markerCssProperty}) 0 ${thickness}px, 
transparent ${thickness}px ${indentWidth}ch)"
```

This creates alternating colored and transparent stripes. Multiple gradients layer together via comma-separated backgrounds to handle active indent highlighting.

## Level Counting & Active Block Logic

The `makeBackgroundCSS` function processes indent entries containing a `level` property and optional `active` property. The algorithm:

1. Skips rendering if `hideFirstIndent` is true and level equals zero
2. Generates inactive markers from the start position up to the active level
3. Inserts a thicker "active" marker at the cursor's indent depth
4. Adds remaining inactive markers after the active one

This creates layered gradients with different thickness values for visual distinction.

## Pseudo-Element Styling

The `::before` pseudo-element applies the gradient layers:

```css
'.cm-indent-markers::before': {
  content: '""',
  position: 'absolute',
  background: 'var(--indent-markers)',
  pointerEvents: 'none',
  zIndex: '-1',
}
```

This overlay sits behind text (z-index: -1) without intercepting mouse events, allowing the line's own background to show while gradient markers render via the pseudo-element.

1	/*
2	 * Outline mode decorations — additive-only indentation (Experiment 1, see
3	 * docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md and the postmortem it
4	 * links). `rem`, not `em`: custom properties resolve at the point of use,
5	 * so an `em`-based unit would inflate under a heading's larger font-size —
6	 * exactly the bug that shipped last time.
7	 *
8	 * Selector specificity: `!important` alone is not enough. Obsidian's own
9	 * app.css has `.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > * { margin: 0px
10	 * !important; }` (three classes) resetting margin on every `.cm-line` —
11	 * our `.cm-line.to-decor-*` (two classes) lost to it outright even with
12	 * `!important` on both sides, confirmed live against a real build. Matching
13	 * the same ancestor structure they use is what wins the cascade, not the
14	 * `!important` keyword by itself.
15	 */
16	
17	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-block {
18	  padding-left: calc(var(--to-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
19	}
20	
21	/* Atoms (code/table/quote/callout/html/hr) render a visible background or
22	 * border box; padding-left only shifts an element's own content, never
23	 * that box, so atoms need margin-left instead. */
24	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-atom {
25	  margin-left: calc(var(--to-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
26	}
27	
28	/* List items: native text-indent/padding-left (the bullet hang) is never
29	 * touched. margin-left is a box-model property native list rendering
30	 * doesn't otherwise use, so adding supplemental depth here is genuinely
31	 * additive on top of untouched native positioning. */
32	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-list {
33	  margin-left: calc(var(--to-supp-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
34	}
35
