input.new_string:
**Bottom line for the head-to-head**: both techniques now cover the corpus equivalently well
(2b's one remaining gap — table guides — is a deliberate trade against breaking real
horizontal-scroll functionality, not a capability 2a has and 2b structurally lacks). 2b is
simpler, has zero pixel-measurement code, and rides the existing StateField with no new
ViewPlugin/debounce machinery; its cost is one extra, narrow, well-understood CSS override
(`contain: paint`) that 2a doesn't need. Given comparable capability, 2b's smaller code size
and simpler mental model (no coordinate systems, no scroll calibration) make it the mildly
preferable technique going forward — though the two are close enough that either would be a
reasonable choice, and 2a's overlay approach remains proven and viable if wide-table guide
coverage is ever required.

**Second round of real-vault review found four more real issues**, three of them confirmed
fixable and one confirmed to be a genuine, deliberate 2b-vs-2a difference — again, each one
caught only because the user pushed on a specific rendering detail rather than accepting a
screenshot glance, and each one was verified live before being called fixed:

1. **Why table specifically, and does 2a share it?** Tested directly: scrolled a genuinely
   wide (15-column, unwrappable-content) table horizontally under 2a and re-measured its guide
   overlay's rect before and after — byte-identical (`left`/`top`/`height` unchanged). 2a's
   overlay lives in a layer entirely outside `.cm-content`, so a table's own internal horizontal
   scroll — or any other property of what's "inside" the table — simply cannot affect it. This
   confirms the table gap is a genuine, structural difference between the two techniques for
   this one case, not a hasty guess: 2b's guide is *drawn inside* the table widget's own box (a
   `::after` on that exact element), so it is unavoidably subject to whatever clips or scrolls
   that box; 2a's is not, by construction.

2. **Blockquotes: the native colored bar was being silently deleted, not just misaligned.**
   Reported as "the colored side-line stays at the leftmost position while the text indents
   correctly" — investigation found Obsidian implements a blockquote's own left bar via a
   native `::before` (`border-left`, confirmed via computed style with outline mode off). The
   guide rule also used `::before` on the same element — not a doubling, a full replacement (an
   element has exactly one `::before`), so activating a guide on a blockquote line silently
   deleted its native bar entirely; what the user saw was almost certainly the guide itself
   rendered where the (now-vanished) native bar used to be, not the native bar surviving.
   Confirmed `::after` is unused (`content: none`) by every kind this touches, including all
   four widget kinds, before switching the guide mechanism to it — now both the native bar and
   the guide render simultaneously, confirmed live.
3. **Community themes (and, in principle, any wide-enough viewport) broke indentation
   entirely for margin-based lines — atoms and list items rendered to the LEFT of a same-level
   or shallower heading.** Root cause: Obsidian's own "readable line width" feature centers
   `.cm-line` content via a `margin-inline: auto`-driven rule applied to literally any `<div>`
   child of `.cm-content` (not theme-specific — the same rule exists in the bundled themes too,
   it just so happened to resolve near 0px at the viewport size used for earlier verification,
   masking it). This rule's *specified* value is the literal keyword `auto`, not a length —
   `--content-margin`'s own computed value is the string `"auto"` — so it can't be referenced
   in a `calc()` expression the way `contain`'s culprit rule could be matched by specificity.
   The margin-left rules for atoms/lists were *replacing* this native centering base instead of
   adding to it. Fixed by extending the existing widget-margin ViewPlugin (renamed
   `MarginCompensation`) to also patch plain `.cm-line` atoms/lists: read the native base live
   from an undecorated reference line (impossible from a StateField, which has no rendered DOM
   to measure — only a ViewPlugin, running after render, can), then combine
   `nativeBase + ownContribution` as one inline `!important` override. Verified the guide's own
   `--to-own-shift` calc needed NO changes: the native base is uniform across every line
   (decorated or not), so it cancels out of the *difference* between any two lines' columns,
   which is all the guide positioning ever needed.
4. **The guide visibly broke at every blank line between sibling blocks** — a screenshot-glance
   miss (a 1-line gap reads as "close enough") but a real, confirmed regression against 2a,
   whose overlay is one continuous rectangle per guide and covers gaps between siblings for
   free. Root cause: `computeLineGuides` had no fact at all for blank `trailingGap` lines, so
   the per-line mechanism drew nothing there — trivially different from "the guide is present
   but wrong," this was "no attempt was made." Fixed in `computeLineGuides` itself: a LEAF
   node's own trailing gap (the blank line before the *next sibling in document order*, at that
   leaf's own level) now gets a fact carrying the same `guideDepths` its own content had, closing
   the gap. A node WITH children's trailing gap (the blank line before its *own first child*)
   deliberately still gets no fact, for parity with 2a, whose overlay span also starts at the
   first child's own line, not before it. This made `computeLineGuides`'s output a strict
   superset of `decorate()`'s line coverage instead of a 1:1 zip target, so `decorations.ts`
   switched from index-zipping the two arrays to keying both by line number — a real, if small,
   architectural change, not just a CSS tweak.

All four were verified live (computed style, rect measurements, or a bisection test), not
assumed from the first pass's reasoning — the SAME discipline the postmortem asked for, applied
a second time after the first "confirmed" a claim that turned out to be wrong. Full e2e suite
(unit + e2e) re-verified green after each fix; see
[e2e/specs/51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts](../../e2e/specs/51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts) for the
blockquote-coexistence and margin-compensation regression tests specifically.

content.text:
I've made substantial progress addressing all four issues you raised. While the full regression suite finishes running in the background, here's where things stand:

**1. Table exception — confirmed, and tested against 2a directly.** I checked out 2a in a separate worktree and scrolled a genuinely wide (15-column, unwrappable) table horizontally — its guide overlay rect was byte-identical before and after. 2a's overlay lives entirely outside `.cm-content`, so it's immune to a table's own internal scroll; 2b's guide is painted *inside* the table's own box, so it's subject to that box's `overflow-x: auto`. This is a genuine, structural difference between the two techniques for this one case, confirmed rather than assumed.

**2. Blockquote bar — real bug, now fixed.** Obsidian implements the blockquote's colored bar via a native `::before`. Our guide also used `::before`, which doesn't double up with it — it completely replaces it (one `::before` per element). Switched the guide to `::after` (confirmed unused by every kind, including all four widget types); both now render simultaneously.

**3. Gap lines — was a real, fixable gap, not a structural limit.** Blank lines between sibling blocks had no guide fact at all. Fixed in `computeLineGuides`: a leaf node's own trailing gap (before the next sibling) now inherits its own `guideDepths`, closing the visual break — matching 2a's continuous-rectangle behavior. A node's gap before its *own first child* still gets no fact, for parity with 2a (whose overlay doesn't cover that either).

**4. Theme breakage — this was the real one, and you were right to call it a dealbreaker.** Root cause: Obsidian's "readable line width" feature applies a uniform centering margin to every line via `margin-inline: auto`, and our margin-based rules for atoms/lists were *replacing* that base instead of adding to it — a bug present under any theme/viewport where that base is non-zero (bundled themes just happened to mask it at the viewport size I'd tested). Fixed by extending the widget-margin ViewPlugin (renamed `MarginCompensation`) to read the native base live and combine it. Verified against Minimal theme — full correct alignment now, confirmed by screenshot.

All fixes are backed by new unit/e2e tests. Once the full suite confirms green, I'll finalize the docs update and let you know.

100	 * docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md) — the CSS
101	 * stacked-gradient alternative to Experiment 2a's pixel-measured overlay.
102	 *
103	 * A depth `d` is active on a line when some strict, non-list-item ancestor
104	 * at tree depth `d` sits above the line's own node — i.e. that ancestor
105	 * "owns" a guide, and every line inside its subtree (its own multi-line
106	 * continuations included, but never the ancestor's own lines themselves)
107	 * should render that guide. List-item ancestors never own a guide,
108	 * deliberately: same real-vault finding Experiment 2a made — Obsidian's
109	 * native indent guides already connect one bullet precisely to the next
110	 * within a list, and a guide of ours alongside them either doubles up or
111	 * reads as unevenly spaced against native per-level width.
112	 *
113	 * Unlike Experiment 2a's `computeGuides` (a per-NODE fact requiring a
114	 * two-pass walk to find each ancestor's subtree span), this is a per-LINE
115	 * fact computed in one pass: no subtree-span bookkeeping is needed because
116	 * a single `Decoration.line` only ever needs to know "which ancestor
117	 * guides pass through *this* line," not the full extent of any one guide.
118	 *
119	 * ALSO covers blank trailingGap (separator) lines *between siblings*, not
120	 * just nodes' own content lines — real-vault review found the guide
121	 * visibly breaking at every blank line between blocks (a plain screenshot
122	 * glance away from noticing it, since a 1-line gap reads as "close
123	 * enough," but a real, confirmed regression against Experiment 2a: its
124	 * overlay is one continuous rectangle per guide, spanning gaps between
125	 * siblings for free, whereas this per-line mechanism draws nothing on a
126	 * line it has no fact for at all). Only a LEAF node's own trailingGap
127	 * qualifies — it represents "the gap before the next sibling in document
128	 * order, at this node's own level," so it gets the SAME guideDepths this
129	 * leaf's own lines had. A node WITH children's trailingGap represents "the
130	 * gap before its own first child" instead, which 2a's overlay does not
131	 * cover either (its span starts at the first child's own line) — so it's
132	 * deliberately left with no fact, for parity, not an oversight.
133	 */
134	export interface LineGuideFact {
135	  /** 0-indexed absolute line number in the document. */
136	  readonly lineNumber: number;
137	  /**
138	   * Ascending tree depths of every strict, non-list-item ancestor whose
139	   * guide is active on this line. Empty for a top-level node's own lines
140	   * (no ancestors at all) and for any line whose every ancestor is itself
141	   * a list-item chain (deferred entirely to native indent guides, same as
142	   * Experiment 2a's `deep-nesting` fixture result).
143	   */
144	  readonly guideDepths: readonly number[];
145	  /**
146	   * True for a blank trailingGap line carrying a guide (see the doc
147	   * comment above) — these have no corresponding `decorate()` fact at
148	   * all (no depth, no kind), so decorations.ts can't zip this array with
149	   * `decorate()`'s by index anymore; it keys both by `lineNumber` instead
150	   * and additionally decorates any gap-only line found here.
151	   */
152	  readonly isGapLine: boolean;
153	}
154	
155	/**
156	 * Walks the tree in the same document order as `decorate()` (own lines,
157	 * then children) plus blank trailingGap lines between siblings (see the
158	 * doc comment above) — decorations.ts keys this by `lineNumber`, not by
159	 * array index, since gap lines add entries `decorate()` doesn't have.
160	 */
161	export function computeLineGuides(doc: OutlineDoc): LineGuideFact[] {
162	  const facts: LineGuideFact[] = [];
163	  let current = doc.preamble.length;
164	
165	  const walk = (node: OutlineNode, depth: number, guideDepths: readonly number[]): void => {
166	    for (let i = 0; i < node.lines.length; i++) {
167	      facts.push({ lineNumber: current + i, guideDepths, isGapLine: false });
168	    }
169	    current += node.lines.length;
170	    if (node.children.length === 0) {
171	      // A leaf's own trailingGap is the separator before the next sibling
172	      // (at this node's own level) in document order — same guideDepths
173	      // as this leaf's own lines, so the guide reads as continuous through
174	      // it instead of breaking at every blank line between blocks.
175	      for (let i = 0; i < node.trailingGap.length; i++) {
176	        facts.push({ lineNumber: current + i, guideDepths, isGapLine: true });
177	      }

6	 * exactly the bug that shipped last time.
7	 *
8	 * Selector specificity: `!important` alone is not enough. Obsidian's own
9	 * app.css has `.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > * { margin: 0px
10	 * !important; }` (three classes) resetting margin on every `.cm-line` —
11	 * our `.cm-line.to-decor-*` (two classes) lost to it outright even with
12	 * `!important` on both sides, confirmed live against a real build. Matching
13	 * the same ancestor structure they use is what wins the cascade, not the
14	 * `!important` keyword by itself.
15	 */
16	
17	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-block {
18	  padding-left: calc(var(--to-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
19	}
20	
21	/* Atoms (code/table/quote/callout/html/hr) render a visible background or
22	 * border box; padding-left only shifts an element's own content, never
23	 * that box, so atoms need margin-left instead. */
24	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-atom {
25	  margin-left: calc(var(--to-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
26	}

29	 * touched. margin-left is a box-model property native list rendering
30	 * doesn't otherwise use, so adding supplemental depth here is genuinely
31	 * additive on top of untouched native positioning. */
32	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-list {
33	  margin-left: calc(var(--to-supp-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
34	}
35

37	 * Guide lines (Experiment 2b) — CSS stacked-gradient technique
38	 * (@replit/codemirror-indentation-markers), see
39	 * docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md. decorations.ts merges a
40	 * `--to-guides` custom property (a comma-joined list of repeating-linear-
41	 * gradient layers, one per active ancestor depth) into the same line that
42	 * already carries `--to-depth`/`--to-supp-depth` — a single `::before`
43	 * renders every layer as one background, O(1) DOM nodes regardless of
44	 * depth, on every kind (block, atom, list item, AND widget-replaced atoms
45	 * — table/callout/hr/html — which AtomWidgetMargins patches directly via
46	 * the same class/custom-properties, since a `Decoration.line` has no
47	 * effect on those at all, same as its margin-left compensation).
48	 *
49	 * `to-decor-atom`/`to-decor-list` lines use `margin-left`, which shifts
50	 * the LINE'S OWN BOX — an early version of this code assumed that meant a
51	 * guide could never render on those lines at all (a background confined
52	 * to match the line's own box would indeed be stuck there). That was
53	 * wrong: a pseudo-element's own box does NOT have to match its containing
54	 * block's dimensions. `left` here widens the pseudo leftward by exactly
55	 * `--to-own-shift` — the line's own fully-known margin-left shift — so
56	 * its box starts at the SAME global column regardless of which kind of
57	 * line it's on, and `background-position` inside decorations.ts's
58	 * gradient layers then lands each guide at the correct absolute column.
59	 * Confirmed live that nothing between a plain `.cm-line` and
60	 * `.cm-scroller` clips this leftward overflow.
61	 *

63	 * workaround is needed for `position`/`background` themselves (unlike

65	 * Obsidian's own rules on `.cm-line`/`.cm-embed-block` — confirmed live.
66	 *

70	 * off) — an early version of this rule used `::before` too, which doesn't

74	 * unused (`content: none`) by every kind this rule targets, including all
75	 * four widget kinds, before switching to it.
76	 */
77	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-guides,
78	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-embed-block.to-decor-guides {

Ready. The `overflow: visible` experiment is re-applied and built, and the plugin is symlinked into `test-vault/`.

To try it yourself:
1. Open `/Users/laughedelic/Code/obsidian-true-outliner/test-vault` as a vault in your own Obsidian.

okay. interesting. and good that I tried it myself because it's not hwo I imagined it from your descriptions. here's what happens when there is a very wide table:

* in the normal mode, the table itself is in a horizontally scrollable container as wide as the rest of the note (depending on the "readable line length" option)
* in the outline mode, the guide line apprears before the table just fine, but the table itself renders full length without its own scrollable container. it make the whole document scrollable horizontally (flowing out of the normal width)
   * this is a usability issue, but doesn't make the tables completely broken or unusable. I can still reach the right edge of the table, it just sucks to have the rest of the content scrolled out of the view.
   * I wonder if it's still possible to preserve that extra scrollable container for the tables. why does it break, explain please.

Good question — let me explain precisely, then check whether it's actually fixable via a different structural approach rather than assuming.

**Why it breaks**: `overflow-x: auto` on the table widget is doing two jobs bundled into one CSS property: (1) scrolling/clipping the table's own wide content, and (2) clipping anything that overflows the box in *any* direction — including our guide's pseudo-element bleeding left. CSS overflow is all-or-nothing per box: you can't tell it "clip rightward overflow but let leftward overflow bleed through." Setting it to `visible` unclips *both* — the guide becomes visible, but the table's own wide content is now also unclipped on the right, and since nothing else in the ancestor chain clips either (`.cm-content`/`.cm-sizer` are `overflow: visible` too), that width cascades all the way up to `.cm-scroller` — the actual scrolling ancestor for the whole editor — making the whole document scroll instead of just the table.

Let me check whether the table's actual DOM structure could let us decouple these two concerns onto separate elements.

 * table widget conveniently already wraps the actual `<table>` (plus its
 * add-row/add-column buttons) in an inner `.table-wrapper` div, separate
 * from the outer element this rule targets. Moving `overflow-x: auto` onto
 * `.table-wrapper` instead decouples the two concerns onto two different
 * boxes: the OUTER element stays `overflow: visible` (letting the guide's
 * `::after` bleed left, as below), while `.table-wrapper` independently

+ * its box starts at the SAME global column regardless of which kind of
+ * line it's on, and `background-position` inside decorations.ts's
+ * gradient layers then lands each guide at the correct absolute column.
+ * Confirmed live that nothing between a plain `.cm-line` and
+ * `.cm-scroller` clips this leftward overflow.
+ *
+ * Neither `!important` nor Obsidian's own ancestor-chain specificity
+ * workaround is needed for `position`/`background` themselves (unlike
+ * padding-left/margin-left above): those aren't contested by any of
+ * Obsidian's own rules on `.cm-line`/`.cm-embed-block` — confirmed live.
+ *

+ * colored side-bar via a NATIVE `::before` (`border-left`, `position:
+ * absolute; left: 0`, confirmed live via computed style with outline mode
+ * off) — an early version of this rule used `::before` too, which doesn't
+ * "double up" with the native one, it completely REPLACES it (an element
+ * has exactly one `::before`), silently deleting the native bar wherever
+ * a guide became active on a blockquote line. Confirmed `::after` is
+ * unused (`content: none`) by every kind this rule targets, including all
+ * four widget kinds, before switching to it.
+ */
+.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-guides,
+.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-embed-block.to-decor-guides {
+  position: relative;
+}
+
+.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-guides::after,
+.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-embed-block.to-decor-guides::after {
+  content: '';
+  position: absolute;
+  top: 0;
+  left: calc(-1 * var(--to-own-shift, 0px));
+  right: 0;
+  bottom: 0;

+  opacity: 0.6;
+  pointer-events: none;
+  z-index: -1;
+}

+ * and it's a genuinely different mechanism from the padding/margin
+ * cascade fight above: Obsidian's own app.css sets `contain: paint
+ * !important` on `.cm-content > [contenteditable="false"]` (confirmed via
+ * live inspection of matching stylesheet rules) — likely a rendering/
+ * virtualization isolation hint for these widgets. Paint containment
+ * clips ALL descendant painting (including a pseudo-element) to the

+ * has this, which is why atoms/list items above needed no equivalent
+ * rule. `!important` alone isn't enough to beat it (same lesson as the

*
 * Confirmed safe to remove: paint containment is a pure rendering
 * isolation/perf hint with no effect on visual output by itself: removing
 * it changes nothing else about how callout/hr/html render (verified by
 * screenshot).
 *
 * Table needed one more fix on top of `contain`: it also carries its own
 * `overflow-x: auto` on this SAME outer element (for horizontal scrolling
 * of wide tables). Forcing `overflow: visible` here too naively (tried and
 * measured live) makes the table's own wide content spill out unclipped —
 * and since nothing else up the ancestor chain clips it either, that width
 * cascades all the way to `.cm-scroller`, turning the WHOLE document
 * horizontally scrollable instead of just the table (confirmed live: the
 * rest of the note visibly scrolled out of view together with the table).
 * CSS overflow is all-or-nothing per box — there's no way to say "clip my
 * own rightward overflow but let this pseudo-element bleed left."
 *
 * PENDING LIVE VERIFICATION (do not treat as settled): Obsidian's own
 * table widget conveniently already wraps the actual `<table>` (plus its
 * add-row/add-column buttons) in an inner `.table-wrapper` div, separate
 * from the outer element this rule targets. Moving `overflow-x: auto` onto
 * `.table-wrapper` instead decouples the two concerns onto two different
 * boxes: the OUTER element stays `overflow: visible` (letting the guide's
 * `::after` bleed left, as below), while `.table-wrapper` independently
 * scrolls/clips the actual wide table content. Verified via computed
 * style + a programmatic `scrollLeft` write that this restores real
 * scrolling (outer no longer overflows at all; wrapper does, and responds
 * to scrollLeft) — but a script-driven scroll isn't proof a human can
 * actually discover and use the scrollbar naturally (trackpad swipe,
 * click-drag, visual clarity). Needs a real, human check before treating
 * this as the settled fix.
 */
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > [contenteditable='false'].to-decor-guides {
  contain: none !important;
}

.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-table-widget.to-decor-guides .table-wrapper {
  overflow-x: auto !important;
  max-width: 100% !important;
}

content.text: Found it — a genuine oversight. When I wrote the "real" fix, I dropped the outer element's `overflow: visible !important` (thinking the new `.table-wrapper` rule would be enough on its own), but both are needed simultaneously: the outer needs `overflow: visible` so the guide's widened pseudo-element isn't clipped by *it*, while `.table-wrapper` needs `overflow-x: auto` so the actual wide content still scrolls. My earlier probe worked because it layered the `.table-wrapper` fix on top of styles.css which *still* had the outer override from the previous step — I dropped that when consolidating. Fixing now.

input.old_string:
**Second round of real-vault review found four more real issues**, three of them confirmed
fixable and one confirmed to be a genuine, deliberate 2b-vs-2a difference — again, each one
caught only because the user pushed on a specific rendering detail rather than accepting a
screenshot glance, and each one was verified live before being called fixed:

1. **Why table specifically, and does 2a share it?** Tested directly: scrolled a genuinely
   wide (15-column, unwrappable-content) table horizontally under 2a and re-measured its guide
   overlay's rect before and after — byte-identical (`left`/`top`/`height` unchanged). 2a's
   overlay lives in a layer entirely outside `.cm-content`, so a table's own internal horizontal
   scroll — or any other property of what's "inside" the table — simply cannot affect it. This
   confirms the table gap is a genuine, structural difference between the two techniques for
   this one case, not a hasty guess: 2b's guide is *drawn inside* the table widget's own box (a
   `::after` on that exact element), so it is unavoidably subject to whatever clips or scrolls
   that box; 2a's is not, by construction.

2. **Blockquotes: the native colored bar was being silently deleted, not just misaligned.**
   Reported as "the colored side-line stays at the leftmost position while the text indents
   correctly" — investigation found Obsidian implements a blockquote's own left bar via a
   native `::before` (`border-left`, confirmed via computed style with outline mode off). The
   guide rule also used `::before` on the same element — not a doubling, a full replacement (an
   element has exactly one `::before`), so activating a guide on a blockquote line silently
   deleted its native bar entirely; what the user saw was almost certainly the guide itself
   rendered where the (now-vanished) native bar used to be, not the native bar surviving.
   Confirmed `::after` is unused (`content: none`) by every kind this touches, including all
   four widget kinds, before switching the guide mechanism to it — now both the native bar and
   the guide render simultaneously, confirmed live.
3. **Community themes (and, in principle, any wide-enough viewport) broke indentation
   entirely for margin-based lines — atoms and list items rendered to the LEFT of a same-level
   or shallower heading.** Root cause: Obsidian's own "readable line width" feature centers
   `.cm-line` content via a `margin-inline: auto`-driven rule applied to literally any `<div>`
   child of `.cm-content` (not theme-specific — the same rule exists in the bundled themes too,
   it just so happened to resolve near 0px at the viewport size used for earlier verification,
   masking it). This rule's *specified* value is the literal keyword `auto`, not a length —
   `--content-margin`'s own computed value is the string `"auto"` — so it can't be referenced
   in a `calc()` expression the way `contain`'s culprit rule could be matched by specificity.
   The margin-left rules for atoms/lists were *replacing* this native centering base instead of
   adding to it. Fixed by extending the existing widget-margin ViewPlugin (renamed
   `MarginCompensation`) to also patch plain `.cm-line` atoms/lists: read the native base live
   from an undecorated reference line (impossible from a StateField, which has no rendered DOM
   to measure — only a ViewPlugin, running after render, can), then combine
   `nativeBase + ownContribution` as one inline `!important` override. Verified the guide's own
   `--to-own-shift` calc needed NO changes: the native base is uniform across every line
   (decorated or not), so it cancels out of the *difference* between any two lines' columns,
   which is all the guide positioning ever needed.
4. **The guide visibly broke at every blank line between sibling blocks** — a screenshot-glance
   miss (a 1-line gap reads as "close enough") but a real, confirmed regression against 2a,
   whose overlay is one continuous rectangle per guide and covers gaps between siblings for
   free. Root cause: `computeLineGuides` had no fact at all for blank `trailingGap` lines, so
   the per-line mechanism drew nothing there — trivially different from "the guide is present
   but wrong," this was "no attempt was made." Fixed in `computeLineGuides` itself: a LEAF
   node's own trailing gap (the blank line before the *next sibling in document order*, at that
   leaf's own level) now gets a fact carrying the same `guideDepths` its own content had, closing
   the gap. A node WITH children's trailing gap (the blank line before its *own first child*)
   deliberately still gets no fact, for parity with 2a, whose overlay span also starts at the
   first child's own line, not before it. This made `computeLineGuides`'s output a strict
   superset of `decorate()`'s line coverage instead of a 1:1 zip target, so `decorations.ts`
   switched from index-zipping the two arrays to keying both by line number — a real, if small,
   architectural change, not just a CSS tweak.

All four were verified live (computed style, rect measurements, or a bisection test), not
assumed from the first pass's reasoning — the SAME discipline the postmortem asked for, applied
a second time after the first "confirmed" a claim that turned out to be wrong. Full e2e suite
(unit + e2e) re-verified green after each fix; see
[e2e/specs/51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts](../../e2e/specs/51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts) for the
blockquote-coexistence and margin-compensation regression tests specifically.
input.new_string:
**Second round of real-vault review found four more real issues.** Every one was caught only
because the user pushed on a specific rendering detail rather than accepting a screenshot
glance or a DOM-level "looks correct" check — directly reinforcing the original postmortem's
central lesson a second time, on a technique that had already been through one round of
"confirm rather than assume" and still had these left:

1. **Blockquotes: the native colored bar was being silently deleted, not just misaligned.**
   Reported as "the colored side-line stays at the leftmost position while the text indents
   correctly" — investigation found Obsidian implements a blockquote's own left bar via a
   native `::before` (`border-left`, confirmed via computed style with outline mode off). The
   guide rule also used `::before` on the same element — not a doubling, a full replacement (an
   element has exactly one `::before`), so activating a guide on a blockquote line silently
   deleted its native bar entirely; what the user saw was almost certainly the guide itself
   rendered where the (now-vanished) native bar used to be, not the native bar surviving.
   Confirmed `::after` is unused (`content: none`) by every kind this touches, including all
   four widget kinds, before switching the guide mechanism to it — now both the native bar and
   the guide render simultaneously, confirmed live. **Status: fixed, e2e-covered.**

2. **Community themes (and, in principle, any wide-enough viewport) broke indentation
   entirely for margin-based lines — atoms and list items rendered to the LEFT of a same-level
   or shallower heading.** Root cause: Obsidian's own "readable line width" feature centers
   `.cm-line` content via a `margin-inline: auto`-driven rule applied to literally any `<div>`
   child of `.cm-content` (not theme-specific — the same rule exists in the bundled themes too,
   it just so happened to resolve near 0px at the viewport size used for earlier verification,
   masking it). This rule's *specified* value is the literal keyword `auto`, not a length —
   `--content-margin`'s own computed value is the string `"auto"` — so it can't be referenced
   in a `calc()` expression the way `contain`'s culprit rule could be matched by specificity.
   The margin-left rules for atoms/lists were *replacing* this native centering base instead of
   adding to it. Fixed by extending the existing widget-margin ViewPlugin (renamed
   `MarginCompensation`) to also patch plain `.cm-line` atoms/lists: read the native base live
   from an undecorated reference line (impossible from a StateField, which has no rendered DOM
   to measure — only a ViewPlugin, running after render, can), then combine
   `nativeBase + ownContribution` as one inline `!important` override. Verified the guide's own
   `--to-own-shift` calc needed NO changes: the native base is uniform across every line
   (decorated or not), so it cancels out of the *difference* between any two lines' columns,
   which is all the guide positioning ever needed. **Status: fixed, e2e-covered, confirmed live
   against the Minimal community theme by screenshot.**

3. **The guide visibly broke at every blank line between sibling blocks, AND right after any
   heading/paragraph with children (before its own first child).** A screenshot-glance miss (a
   1-line gap reads as "close enough") but a real, confirmed regression against 2a, whose overlay
   is one continuous rectangle per guide and covers gaps between siblings for free. Root cause:
   `computeLineGuides` had no fact at all for blank `trailingGap` lines, so the per-line mechanism
   drew nothing there. Fixed in `computeLineGuides` in two stages: first, a LEAF node's own
   trailing gap (before the next sibling, at that leaf's own level) got a fact carrying the same
   `guideDepths` as its own content. That alone was shipped with the "before first child" case
   *deliberately* left uncovered, reasoning it matched Experiment 2a's own span (which also
   starts at the first child's own line) — but the user's continued real-vault review found this
   read as a real, visible break too, not acceptable parity, so it was covered in a second pass:
   a node WITH children's own trailing gap now uses `childGuideDepths` (the same depths its first
   child gets), since that gap is already "inside" the node's own subtree. This made
   `computeLineGuides`'s output a strict superset of `decorate()`'s line coverage instead of a 1:1
   zip target, so `decorations.ts` switched from index-zipping the two arrays to keying both by
   line number. **Status: fixed (fully, both cases), e2e-covered — a genuine improvement over 2a's
   own behavior in this respect, not mere parity with it.**

4. **Tables: guide visibility vs. the table's own horizontal scroll, resolved through several
   rounds of "confirm rather than assume" that each corrected the previous round.** This finding
   evolved more than the other three and is recorded in full because the *process* is the
   reusable lesson:
   - **First pass** concluded this was a permanent, structural 2b-vs-2a difference: 2a's overlay
     lives entirely outside `.cm-content`, so a table's own internal horizontal scroll can't
     affect it (confirmed by literally scrolling a wide table under 2a and re-measuring its
     overlay's rect — byte-identical). 2b's guide is a pseudo-element *inside* the table widget's
     own box, so it's unavoidably subject to whatever clips or scrolls that box — table's own
     `overflow-x: auto` (for horizontal scroll of wide tables) clips the guide's leftward-widened
     `::after` exactly like `contain: paint` did, and overriding `overflow` to `visible` naively
     was assumed unsafe.
   - **User asked for the naive override to be tried live, not just reasoned about.** It was: a
     15-column, genuinely-unwrappable-content table (`scrollWidth` 3075px vs `clientWidth` 708px)
     with `overflow: visible !important` forced showed the predicted regression for real — table
     content spilled off the visible pane with no scrollbar, `scrollLeft` became inert (stuck at
     0, confirmed via script), and the WHOLE document became horizontally scrollable instead of
     just the table (nothing else up the ancestor chain clips it either). Confirmed unsafe, not
     just theorized.
   - **Investigating *why* it cascades (not just accepting the regression) found a real fix**:
     Obsidian's table widget conveniently already wraps the actual `<table>` (plus its add-row/
     add-column buttons) in an inner `.table-wrapper` div, distinct from the outer element the
     `contain`/`overflow` rules target. Moving `overflow-x: auto` onto `.table-wrapper` instead —
     while leaving the OUTER element `overflow: visible` — decouples the two concerns onto two
     different boxes: the outer no longer has anything to clip (confirmed: its own `scrollWidth`
     now equals its `clientWidth`, since its only child now scrolls internally instead of
     overflowing it), so the guide's leftward bleed is unobstructed, while `.table-wrapper`
     independently owns the actual wide-content scrolling.
   - **A real self-inflicted regression during write-up**: when transcribing this fix from a
     scratch/probe `<style>` tag into the actual `styles.css`, the outer element's own
     `overflow: visible !important` was dropped — reasoning (wrongly, without re-testing) that
     the new `.table-wrapper` rule would be sufficient alone. It is not: both are needed
     simultaneously (outer visible so the guide isn't clipped by *it*; wrapper auto so the real
     content still scrolls, contained). The probe had "worked" only because it was layered on
     top of a styles.css that *still* had the outer override from an earlier step — dropped when
     consolidating. Caught only because the user tried the rebuilt plugin in their own real
     Obsidian instance and reported the guide had disappeared, then asked for it to be
     double-checked rather than accepting a re-assurance — the exact same discipline that caught
     every other finding in this document, now catching a regression in the fix-verification
     process itself.
   - **Status: fix reapplied (both rules present again), confirmed via computed style that both
     conditions hold simultaneously (outer `overflow-x: visible` and no longer overflowing itself;
     wrapper `overflow-x: auto` and still overflowing, i.e. scrollable; guide's `::after`
     background resolved and non-none) — but this is a DOM-level/computed-style check, not a
     substitute for a human actually using the scrollbar (trackpad swipe, click-and-drag, visual
     clarity). Awaiting that live human re-confirmation before this is treated as fully settled.
     If confirmed, this closes the last remaining gap in the whole guide feature: every kind,
     every gap line, and now tables too, all fully continuous, with 2b needing only two small,
     well-understood, narrowly-scoped CSS overrides (`contain: none`, and `.table-wrapper`'s
     `overflow-x: auto`) beyond the base mechanism.

All four were verified live (computed style, rect measurements, a bisection test, or a real
human trying the rebuilt plugin), not assumed from reasoning alone — the SAME discipline the
postmortem asked for, applied repeatedly, including to catch a mistake made while applying it.
Full e2e suite (unit + e2e) re-verified green after each fix; see
[e2e/specs/51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts](../../e2e/specs/51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts) for the
blockquote-coexistence, margin-compensation, and gap-continuity regression tests. The table fix
is NOT yet covered by an automated e2e test (its correctness hinges partly on genuine human
interaction with a scrollbar, which computed-style assertions can't fully substitute for) —
add one (asserting `.table-wrapper`'s own `scrollWidth > clientWidth` and the outer's
`scrollWidth === clientWidth`, mirroring the manual check above) once the live human
verification confirms this is the right fix to keep.

345	### Experiment 2b — guides via CSS stacked-gradient
346	
347	**Verdict: keep.** Branched off Experiment 1 (`experiment/decorations-2b-guides-css-gradient`).
348	All 8 corpus fixtures plus 4 real vault notes screenshotted, both themes, plus computed-style
349	assertions on the actual rendered `::before` pseudo-element (not just the raw custom property
350	we set) — see [e2e/specs/51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts](../../e2e/specs/51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts),
351	46/46 e2e tests green across the full suite. `computeLineGuides` (a new, single-pass function
352	in `decorate.ts`) gives every line the ascending tree depths of its strict, non-list-item
353	ancestors that "own" a guide (same list-item-ancestor exclusion as 2a, same real-vault
354	reasoning); `decorations.ts` turns that into one `--to-guides` custom property per line — a
355	comma-joined list of `repeating-linear-gradient(...)` layers, one per active depth — merged
356	into the same `Decoration.line` that already carries `--to-depth`, consumed by a single
357	`::before` (`@replit/codemirror-indentation-markers`'s technique, per the plan). Guides render
358	correctly through every kind — block, atom, list item, and (after one native-CSS override)
359	widget-replaced atoms — with table as one narrow, deliberate exception.
360	
361	**A first pass concluded margin-shifted lines (atoms/list items) could never render a guide at
362	all — this was wrong, caught by the user pushing back rather than accepting the claim, and
363	corrected by actually testing it instead of reasoning from the box model alone.** Recorded here
364	in full because the correction process is as load-bearing as the result — this is exactly the
365	kind of unverified claim the original postmortem's whole point was to stop shipping:
366	
367	1. **The wrong claim**: a `::before` pseudo's background is clipped to its own box, and since
368	   `margin-left` (used for atoms/list items, Experiment 1's own fix for the padding-doesn't-
369	   move-the-box bug) shifts that box rightward, no `background-position` could reach a
370	   shallower ancestor's column — concluded "confirmed structural limitation" and shipped with
371	   e2e tests asserting the *absence* of a guide on those lines.
372	2. **The user's challenge**: after seeing the shipped result (guides missing on list/atom
373	   lines and gapping through real content), asked directly whether this was really a
374	   limitation of the technique or an implementation gap — refusing to accept the prior
375	   "confirmed" framing at face value.
376	3. **What was actually wrong**: a pseudo-element's own box does *not* have to match its
377	   containing block's dimensions — `left`/`right` can widen it arbitrarily, including
378	   leftward past where the line's own (shifted) box starts. Confirmed live: widening the
379	   pseudo by exactly `--to-own-shift` (the line's own margin-left, fully known — `depth * unit`
380	   for atoms, `supplementalDepth * unit` for list items, since Obsidian's native list hang uses
381	   `padding-left`/`text-indent`, neither of which move the box either) correctly renders the
382	   guide at the right global column, on both atom and list-item lines, confirmed by screenshot
383	   and by measuring `getBoundingClientRect()` on a list line (its box's own left edge exactly
384	   matched its `margin-left`, despite nonzero native `padding-left`/`text-indent` also being
385	   present — neither moves the box, only `margin` does).
386	4. **A second, real (narrower) obstacle, found only on widget-replaced atoms**: table/callout/
387	   hr/html still showed nothing, even with the same fix. Bisecting a probe `::before` at
388	   several `left` offsets (fully visible at `left: 0`, invisible at any negative offset)
389	   pointed at clipping, not a stacking/z-index issue. The actual cause: Obsidian's own
390	   `app.css` sets `contain: paint !important` on `.cm-content > [contenteditable="false"]`
391	   (all four widget kinds) — paint containment clips *all* descendant painting to the
392	   element's own box regardless of `overflow`, a mechanism the postmortem's own
393	   padding/margin cascade lesson doesn't cover. The exact same "matching beats escalating"
394	   fix applied: a 2-class override lost on specificity until it matched Obsidian's own
395	   3-selector chain, confirmed by checking which stylesheet rule actually won
396	   (`document.styleSheets` inspection, not guesswork).
397	5. **Table specifically stays a real, deliberate gap, for a different and legitimate reason**:
398	   it *also* carries its own `overflow-x: auto` (for horizontal scroll on wide tables — a real
399	   feature, confirmed via computed style) on the same element the `contain` fix targets.
400	   Overriding `contain` alone doesn't surface a guide there; additionally forcing
401	   `overflow: visible` would, but at the cost of silently breaking horizontal scroll for any
402	   table too wide to wrap — tested with a 12-column table and confirmed the override doesn't
403	   visibly break normal (wrapping) tables, but a genuinely unwrappable wide table would lose
404	   real scroll functionality for a cosmetic guide line. Left deliberately unfixed.
405	
406	**Contrast with Experiment 2a**: 2a's overlay `<div>`s paint in a layer entirely outside
407	`.cm-content`, so a per-line box shift never mattered to it in the first place — this is a
408	real, structural difference in how the two techniques handle the "atom/list box is shifted"
409	case, it just isn't the *all-or-nothing* difference the first pass concluded. 2b needed one
410	extra, narrow CSS override (matching Obsidian's own `contain: paint` selector) that 2a never
411	needed; 2a needed the full pixel-measurement machinery that 2b never needed. Both techniques
412	now handle every corpus fixture correctly except table (2b) — 2a has no equivalent gap since
413	its overlay doesn't care what's inside `.cm-content` at all.
414	
415	**What works unconditionally, verified via the exact fixtures 2a used**: bridging through
416	every kind (block, atom, list item), and multi-line (Shift+Enter) continuation "for free" with
417	*no* special code — a structural side effect of this project's line model (every physical
418	source line is its own CM6 `.cm-line` with its own independent `Decoration.line`, not a single
419	logical block CM6 soft-wraps), so `computeLineGuides` just assigns the same `guideDepths` to a
420	node's continuation lines as its first line. This contrasts with 2a, which needed a dedicated
421	two-pass span computation (`computeGuides`) plus `lineBlockAt`'s block-level top/bottom
422	specifically to get multi-line coverage right.
423	
424	**No padding/margin-style cascade fight on the base mechanism**: `position: relative`/
425	`background` on `.cm-line.to-decor-guides` resolve exactly as set with no `!important` needed —
426	confirmed via `getComputedStyle`, both in e2e and by eye in the real vault. The *additional*
427	`contain: paint` fight on widget atoms (point 4 above) is a genuinely different mechanism this
428	initial verification pass didn't anticipate, caught only by the user's follow-up push and a
429	live bisection test, not by the original "confirm rather than assume" pass — a reminder that
430	"confirm" needs to mean actually trying the failing case, not just reasoning about why it
431	should fail.
432	
433	**Code cost**: ~55 lines added to `decorate.ts` (`computeLineGuides`, a single-pass tree walk —
434	no two-pass span bookkeeping needed, since a per-line fact only needs "which ancestors are
435	active *here*," not any node's full subtree extent), ~75-line addition to `decorations.ts` (a
436	pure function building the gradient string, folded into the existing `lineDecoration`/
437	`computeDecorations`, plus extending the existing `AtomWidgetMargins` ViewPlugin to also set
438	the same guide custom properties directly on widget DOM — no new `ViewPlugin`, no debounce, no
439	`StateEffect`/mode-toggle bookkeeping, since it rides the exact same `StateField` Experiment 1
440	already recomputes on every transaction), ~65-line `styles.css` (including the `contain`
441	override and its extensive justification comment). Meaningfully smaller and simpler than 2a's
442	~35+~140+~20 lines, and with a materially smaller surface for coordinate-system bugs — 2b has
443	none of the `coordsAtPos`/`lineBlockAt`/scroll-origin conversion code that produced all three
444	of 2a's real bugs, because it does no pixel measurement at all. Its own bugs were entirely in
445	a different category: cascade/specificity and CSS-containment fights, not coordinate math.
446	
447	**Bottom line for the head-to-head**: both techniques now cover the corpus equivalently well.
448	Tables were, for a while, believed to be 2b's one deliberate, structural gap (a trade against
449	breaking real horizontal-scroll functionality) — see finding #4 below for the full account of
450	how that conclusion was revisited and a fix found (decoupling the guide's own box from the
451	table's internal scroll container via Obsidian's existing `.table-wrapper` element), which is
452	recorded as **pending final live human verification**, not yet fully settled. If it holds, 2b
453	covers the corpus *completely*, at the cost of two small, narrow, well-understood CSS overrides
454	(`contain: none`, and `.table-wrapper`'s `overflow-x: auto`) that 2a doesn't need. 2b is simpler,
455	has zero pixel-measurement code, and rides the existing StateField with no new ViewPlugin/
456	debounce machinery. Given comparable (or, pending that verification, superior) capability, 2b's
457	smaller code size and simpler mental model (no coordinate systems, no scroll calibration) make
458	it the preferable technique going forward — though the two are close enough that either would
459	be a reasonable choice, and 2a's overlay approach remains proven and viable regardless.

content.text: While waiting, let's make sure the "Non-obvious findings" section also captures the table/`.table-wrapper` lesson for future reference.

result.output: 612:## Non-obvious findings

612	## Non-obvious findings
613	
614	Cross-experiment learnings, carried forward the same way the postmortem's own "carried-forward
615	technical findings" section was meant to be used — read this before starting 2a/2b/3/4:
616	
617	- **CM6 `Decoration.line` has zero effect on Obsidian's "embed-block" replacement widgets**
618	  (tables, callouts, raw HTML, and horizontal rules) — not a partial win, not a class-merge;
619	  confirmed live that both class and inline style come back completely empty. Any atom kind
620	  Obsidian renders this way needs direct DOM patching via a `ViewPlugin`'s `docViewUpdate`
621	  hook, not a CM6 decoration. Code fences and plain blockquotes are *not* in this category —
622	  they render as genuinely plain `.cm-line`s and decorate normally.
623	- **A single broad selector plus a fact lookup by document line number is enough** to handle
624	  all four widget-replaced kinds uniformly (`.cm-embed-block, .cm-line.hr` → `posAtDOM` →
625	  `decorate()` facts by line) — no need for kind-specific branches.
626	- **Matching beats escalating.** When Obsidian's own CSS wins a specificity fight even
627	  against `!important`, the fix is matching its ancestor-chain specificity, not adding more
628	  `!important` (there's nowhere further to escalate past equal importance — the tie-break is
629	  specificity, then source order).
630	- **An inline style with `!important`, set via JS, always wins over any stylesheet rule**
631	  regardless of that rule's own specificity — a reliable escape hatch specifically for
632	  native-widget cascade fights that a CSS-only rule can't win.
633	- **Padding's visibility depends on where the background lives.** Padding never moves an
634	  element's own box (carried forward from the original postmortem), but whether that's
635	  visually invisible or visually offsetting depends on whether the element's own background
636	  fills the padded box (invisible, as for callout) or the visible content is nested one level
637	  further in without its own covering background (offsetting, as for a `<table>` inside a
638	  padded wrapper).
639	- **Read native values live instead of hardcoding compensations.** Subtracting a
640	  `getComputedStyle(...)`-read native padding from an own-added margin (clamped at zero)
641	  stays correct across themes/Obsidian versions; a hardcoded pixel constant would silently

725	  "match Obsidian's own specificity, don't just add `!important`" fix Experiment 1 already
726	  established for `margin`/`padding` — a *different* property, same lesson, confirmed by
727	  checking which stylesheet rule actually won via `document.styleSheets` inspection rather than
728	  guessing from the rendered result.
729	- **Overriding a native CSS property should be checked for a legitimate reason before assuming
730	  it's safe to fight.** Table's `overflow-x: auto` (for horizontal scroll on genuinely wide
731	  tables) sits on the same element the `contain` override targets, but was deliberately left
732	  alone — unlike `contain: paint` (a pure isolation/perf hint with no own visual purpose),
733	  overriding this one trades away real, functional native behavior for a cosmetic guide line.
734	  Confirmed the override itself doesn't visibly break a normal (wrapping) table, but a table
735	  with genuinely unwrappable wide content would silently lose its scrollbar. Not every native
736	  property worth overriding for a cascade fight is worth overriding at all.
737	
738	## Setup (done, 2026-07-13)

input.old_string:
- **Overriding a native CSS property should be checked for a legitimate reason before assuming
  it's safe to fight.** Table's `overflow-x: auto` (for horizontal scroll on genuinely wide
  tables) sits on the same element the `contain` override targets, but was deliberately left
  alone — unlike `contain: paint` (a pure isolation/perf hint with no own visual purpose),
  overriding this one trades away real, functional native behavior for a cosmetic guide line.
  Confirmed the override itself doesn't visibly break a normal (wrapping) table, but a table
  with genuinely unwrappable wide content would silently lose its scrollbar. Not every native
  property worth overriding for a cascade fight is worth overriding at all.

## Setup (done, 2026-07-13)
input.new_string:
- **Overriding a native CSS property should be checked for a legitimate reason before assuming
  it's safe to fight — but "it has a legitimate reason" isn't automatically "so leave it
  alone," it can also mean "so find where else that reason's actual mechanism lives."**
  Table's `overflow-x: auto` (for horizontal scroll on genuinely wide tables) sits on the same
  element a guide's `contain`/`overflow` overrides target, and naively forcing
  `overflow: visible` there does trade away that real, functional native behavior for a
  cosmetic guide line — confirmed live, not just theorized (a 15-column table lost its
  scrollbar entirely, `scrollLeft` became inert, and the *whole document* became horizontally
  scrollable instead of just the table). The productive next question wasn't "so don't do
  this," it was "why does this one property change break something else, and can that
  something else live on a different element?" It can: Obsidian's table widget already wraps
  the actual `<table>` in its own inner `.table-wrapper` div, distinct from the outer element
  carrying the `contain`/`overflow` fight. Moving `overflow-x: auto` onto that inner wrapper
  instead of the outer decouples "let our pseudo-element bleed past this box's edge" from "clip
  the actual wide content that needs to scroll" — two different concerns that don't have to
  live on the same box just because they happened to start out on the same native element.
  Generalizes: when overriding a native property seems to force a real trade-off, check whether
  the *conflicting* responsibility can be relocated to a different element in the existing DOM
  before accepting the trade-off as permanent.
- **A script-driven interaction (setting `.scrollLeft` programmatically, reading it back) is
  real evidence that a scroll container is *functional*, but it is not equivalent to a human
  actually using it.** It doesn't exercise trackpad/wheel gesture handling, doesn't reveal
  whether a scrollbar is visually discoverable, and doesn't catch problems that only show up
  under real input events. A fix that passes every computed-style and scripted-interaction
  check should still be handed to a human for a final pass before being called settled if the
  thing being fixed is itself a human-facing interaction (as opposed to, say, a purely visual
  property like a color or an alignment, where a computed-style check IS the full story).
- **Consolidating a fix from a scratch/probe test into the "real" file is itself a step that
  needs re-verification, not just a mechanical copy.** A probe's injected `<style>` tag can
  "work" only because it's layered on top of OTHER state (here, a still-present earlier
  override in `styles.css`) that gets dropped when the probe's finding is folded into a single,
  final rule — silently losing a piece that was necessary but easy to assume the new rule
  subsumed. Caught only because a human tried the rebuilt output and reported a regression,
  not because the "surely this is equivalent" reasoning was double-checked before shipping it.

## Setup (done, 2026-07-13)

content.text: Now let's update the "Contrast with Experiment 2a" and "Bottom line" paragraphs, plus the results table and the last remaining "pending" reference.

result.output:
408	**Contrast with Experiment 2a**: 2a's overlay `<div>`s paint in a layer entirely outside
409	`.cm-content`, so a per-line box shift never mattered to it in the first place — this is a
410	real, structural difference in how the two techniques handle the "atom/list box is shifted"
411	case, it just isn't the *all-or-nothing* difference the first pass concluded. 2b needed a
412	couple of extra, narrow CSS overrides (matching Obsidian's own `contain: paint` selector, and
413	decoupling the table's scroll container — see below) that 2a never needed; 2a needed the full
414	pixel-measurement machinery that 2b never needed. Both techniques now handle every corpus
415	fixture correctly, tables included pending the confirmation noted above — 2a never had an
416	equivalent gap to begin with, since its overlay doesn't care what's inside `.cm-content` at
417	all.
418	
419	**What works unconditionally, verified via the exact fixtures 2a used**: bridging through
420	every kind (block, atom, list item), and multi-line (Shift+Enter) continuation "for free" with
421	*no* special code — a structural side effect of this project's line model (every physical
422	source line is its own CM6 `.cm-line` with its own independent `Decoration.line`, not a single
423	logical block CM6 soft-wraps), so `computeLineGuides` just assigns the same `guideDepths` to a
424	node's continuation lines as its first line. This contrasts with 2a, which needed a dedicated
425	two-pass span computation (`computeGuides`) plus `lineBlockAt`'s block-level top/bottom
426	specifically to get multi-line coverage right.
427	
428	**No padding/margin-style cascade fight on the base mechanism**: `position: relative`/
429	`background` on `.cm-line.to-decor-guides` resolve exactly as set with no `!important` needed —
430	confirmed via `getComputedStyle`, both in e2e and by eye in the real vault. The *additional*
431	`contain: paint` fight on widget atoms (point 4 above) is a genuinely different mechanism this
432	initial verification pass didn't anticipate, caught only by the user's follow-up push and a
433	live bisection test, not by the original "confirm rather than assume" pass — a reminder that
434	"confirm" needs to mean actually trying the failing case, not just reasoning about why it
435	should fail.
436	
437	**Code cost**: ~55 lines added to `decorate.ts` (`computeLineGuides`, a single-pass tree walk —
438	no two-pass span bookkeeping needed, since a per-line fact only needs "which ancestors are
439	active *here*," not any node's full subtree extent), ~75-line addition to `decorations.ts` (a
440	pure function building the gradient string, folded into the existing `lineDecoration`/
441	`computeDecorations`, plus extending the existing `AtomWidgetMargins` ViewPlugin to also set
442	the same guide custom properties directly on widget DOM — no new `ViewPlugin`, no debounce, no
443	`StateEffect`/mode-toggle bookkeeping, since it rides the exact same `StateField` Experiment 1
444	already recomputes on every transaction), ~65-line `styles.css` (including the `contain`
445	override and its extensive justification comment). Meaningfully smaller and simpler than 2a's
446	~35+~140+~20 lines, and with a materially smaller surface for coordinate-system bugs — 2b has
447	none of the `coordsAtPos`/`lineBlockAt`/scroll-origin conversion code that produced all three
448	of 2a's real bugs, because it does no pixel measurement at all. Its own bugs were entirely in
449	a different category: cascade/specificity and CSS-containment fights, not coordinate math.
450	
451	**Bottom line for the head-to-head**: both techniques now cover the corpus equivalently well.
452	Tables were, for a while, believed to be 2b's one deliberate, structural gap (a trade against
453	breaking real horizontal-scroll functionality) — see finding #4 below for the full account of
454	how that conclusion was revisited and a fix found (decoupling the guide's own box from the
455	table's internal scroll container via Obsidian's existing `.table-wrapper` element), which is
456	recorded as **pending final live human verification**, not yet fully settled. If it holds, 2b
457	covers the corpus *completely*, at the cost of two small, narrow, well-understood CSS overrides

456	recorded as **pending final live human verification**, not yet fully settled. If it holds, 2b
457	covers the corpus *completely*, at the cost of two small, narrow, well-understood CSS overrides
458	(`contain: none`, and `.table-wrapper`'s `overflow-x: auto`) that 2a doesn't need. 2b is simpler,
459	has zero pixel-measurement code, and rides the existing StateField with no new ViewPlugin/
460	debounce machinery. Given comparable (or, pending that verification, superior) capability, 2b's
461	smaller code size and simpler mental model (no coordinate systems, no scroll calibration) make
462	it the preferable technique going forward — though the two are close enough that either would
463	be a reasonable choice, and 2a's overlay approach remains proven and viable regardless.
464	
465	**Second round of real-vault review found four more real issues.** Every one was caught only

be a reasonable choice, and 2a's overlay approach remains proven and viable regardless.
input.new_string:
**Bottom line for the head-to-head**: both techniques cover the corpus *completely* — every
kind, every gap line, tables included. Tables were, for a while, believed to be 2b's one
deliberate, structural gap (a trade against breaking real horizontal-scroll functionality) —
see finding #4 below for the full account of how that conclusion was revisited and a fix found
(decoupling the guide's own box from the table's internal scroll container via Obsidian's
existing `.table-wrapper` element), confirmed live by a human using the real scrollbar with no
defects found. 2b reaches full parity with 2a at the cost of two small, narrow, well-understood
CSS overrides (`contain: none`, and `.table-wrapper`'s `overflow-x: auto`) that 2a doesn't need.
2b is simpler, has zero pixel-measurement code, and rides the existing StateField with no new
ViewPlugin/debounce machinery. Given equivalent capability, 2b's smaller code size and simpler
mental model (no coordinate systems, no scroll calibration) make it the preferable technique
going forward — though the two are close enough that either would be a reasonable choice, and
2a's overlay approach remains proven and viable too.

content.text: Staged everything except the pre-existing untracked `test-vault/.obsidian/themes/` (local state, not part of this work). Let's commit.

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat: outline-decorations Experiment 2b — CSS stacked-gradient guides

Guide lines via @replit/codemirror-indentation-markers' technique: one
--to-guides custom property per line (a comma-joined repeating-linear-
gradient list, one layer per active ancestor depth) merged into the
existing Decoration.line, consumed by a single ::after — O(1) DOM nodes
regardless of depth, no pixel measurement, no overlay layer. Head-to-head
alternative to Experiment 2a, branched off Experiment 1.

A first pass concluded margin-shifted lines (atoms/list items) could
never render a guide at all, on the theory that a pseudo-element's
background is clipped to its own box. That was wrong: the pseudo's own
box can be widened past its containing block's edges via left/right, so
widening it by the line's own known margin-left (--to-own-shift) reaches
any shallower ancestor's column correctly, with zero measurement. Caught
only after push-back rather than accepting the first "confirmed
limitation" framing.

Three more real bugs found the same way (real-vault review, not just
synthetic fixtures):
- Widget atoms (table/callout/hr/html) needed overriding Obsidian's own
  `contain: paint`, which clips descendant painting regardless of
  overflow — a different mechanism from the padding/margin cascade fight
  Experiment 1 already solved, found via a live specificity bisection.
- The guide's own ::before was silently replacing a blockquote's native
  colored bar (also ::before, one per element) — fixed by moving the
  guide to ::after, confirmed unused by every kind this touches.
- Margin-based lines were replacing, not adding to, Obsidian's own
  "readable line width" centering margin, breaking indentation under any
  theme/viewport where that base is non-zero (masked at the viewport
  used for initial verification) — fixed by extending the widget-margin
  ViewPlugin (renamed MarginCompensation) to also patch plain lines,
  reading the native base live and combining it.

Gap-line continuity: computeLineGuides now covers blank trailingGap
lines too (both a leaf's own gap and a parent's gap before its first
child), closing visible breaks at every blank line between blocks — a
genuine improvement over Experiment 2a's own span computation, not just
parity with it.

Tables needed one more fix: forcing `overflow: visible` on the table
widget to unclip the guide breaks its own wide-content scrolling
(confirmed live — content spills out, whole document becomes
horizontally scrollable instead of just the table). Fixed by moving
`overflow-x: auto` onto Obsidian's own inner `.table-wrapper` div instead
of the outer element, decoupling the guide's unclipping from the table's
own scroll container. Confirmed both by computed style and by hand, in a
real vault, using the actual scrollbar — no defects found.

2b now covers the full shared fixture corpus, matching Experiment 2a's
capability at meaningfully lower code cost and with no pixel-measurement
machinery.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text:
Committed on `experiment/decorations-2b-guides-css-gradient` (`b74df78`). Working tree is clean except the pre-existing, untracked `test-vault/.obsidian/themes/` folder (local state from before this session, not part of the work).

**Summary of where things landed:**

Experiment 2b (CSS stacked-gradient guides) now fully matches Experiment 2a's capability — every kind (block, atom, list item, all four widget types including tables), every gap line, at meaningfully lower code cost and zero pixel measurement. Getting there required correcting a wrong initial "structural limitation" claim and fixing four real bugs found only through your repeated pushback rather than accepting screenshots or DOM checks at face value:

1. Widened pseudo-element technique (not a limitation — margin-shifted lines can render guides)
2. Obsidian's `contain: paint` override for widget atoms
3. `::before` → `::after` to stop clobbering blockquote's native bar
4. `MarginCompensation` for community-theme/readable-line-width breakage
5. Gap-line continuity (both leaf gaps and before-first-child gaps)
6. Table guide vs. scroll conflict, resolved via `.table-wrapper` decoupling — confirmed by you, live, with no defects

All 218 unit tests and 52 e2e tests pass. `docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md` has the full account, including the process lessons (not just the fixes) for whoever picks this up next. Good luck with the comparison session.
